turns.content: Why are African national borders so geometric while European ones follow rivers and mountains?

A lot of Africa’s borders look “straight” because many were drawn relatively late (mostly late 19th to mid-20th century) by colonial powers negotiating with each other on maps, often using lines of latitude/longitude, meridians, or simple straight-line segments between a few surveyed points. Those boundaries were frequently decided in European capitals or colonial conferences and then later “fixed” through treaties, with only limited on-the-ground surveying at first. Europe’s borders, by contrast, are the product of many centuries of incremental change—wars, dynastic unions, dissolutions, treaties, and local negotiations—often anchored in physical features (rivers, ridgelines) and long-settled cultural-linguistic frontiers. Mountains and rivers make defensible boundaries and are obvious markers for populations living there, so they frequently became “natural” reference lines. But Europe also has straight borders in places (e.g., parts of Poland’s borders after WWII are quite geometric; and the Belgium–Netherlands border has weird enclaves), and Africa also has borders following rivers and lakes (e.g., Congo River segments; Lake Victoria shorelines). The difference is one of degree and historical timing.
That makes sense, but can you explain the timing piece more? Why would late borders be straighter?

Late border-making often happens under three conditions that encourage straight lines: 1) Cartographic abstraction outpaces ground knowledge: By the late 1800s, European states had global maps with latitude/longitude grids and improved surveying methods, but vast interior regions were still imperfectly mapped in detail. It was easier to agree “the boundary is the 11th parallel north” than to agree on a ridge whose exact course nobody had reliably surveyed. 2) Inter-imperial bargaining favors simple, verifiable terms: When two colonial powers negotiate, they want treaty language that is unambiguous and enforceable. “A line due east from point X to meridian Y” is clear. “The watershed between river basins” is also clear in principle, but only if it’s well mapped. A straight line can be implemented later by survey teams. 3) Administrative convenience: Colonial administrations often drew “spheres of influence” with minimal regard for preexisting polities. Straight lines are quick to draw and easy to administer in a top-down way, even if they cut across ethnic, linguistic, or ecological zones. Earlier borders—in Europe and elsewhere—tended to emerge from local control of land, fortifications, and resource zones. Those were shaped by terrain because terrain shapes movement, defense, agriculture, and settlement. So the timing correlates with the technology and politics of border-making.
But weren’t there also colonial borders in Europe? Like empires too. Yet Europe doesn’t look like a grid.

Europe certainly had empires—Habsburg, Ottoman, Russian, Napoleonic, etc.—but their internal divisions and external frontiers usually evolved through long contestation in relatively dense, well-known terrain. Several factors reduce the “grid” look: - Density of settlement and historical claims: In Europe, even if a line could be drawn geometrically, it would collide with many established jurisdictions, property systems, and communities, making it harder to impose cleanly. - Physical geography is more constraining: Europe’s mountain systems (Alps, Pyrenees, Carpathians, Balkans) and navigable rivers (Rhine, Danube) are strong organizing features for politics and trade. They repeatedly become reference points. - The modern nation-state system formed earlier: Many European borders “crystallized” in phases from the Peace of Westphalia (1648) onward, then through 19th-century nationalism and 20th-century wars. That’s a long time for borders to be “smoothed” by local adjustments, plebiscites, small territorial swaps, and compromises along natural lines. That said, Europe has plenty of borders that are not natural-feature-based: the straight segments of the Czech–Austria border in places, or the Poland–Czech border’s oddities, or the lines created by the Iron Curtain era. It’s just that Africa’s late-19th-century partition produced many long straight segments at once.
People always mention the Berlin Conference. Did that literally draw borders with rulers?

The 1884–85 Berlin Conference is often simplified as “they drew all the borders there,” but it was more about setting rules for claiming territory—especially “effective occupation”—and managing rivalries among European powers. Many specific borders were negotiated in subsequent bilateral treaties between Britain, France, Germany, Portugal, Belgium, Italy, and Spain. However, the conference and the broader “Scramble for Africa” era did encourage map-based bargaining. Colonial diplomats and cartographers did use straight lines, geometric arcs, and coordinate-based descriptions because those were practical for treaty text and for dividing “spheres of influence” quickly. So while Berlin didn’t singlehandedly sketch the continent, it helped create the diplomatic machinery and incentives that made geometric borders common.
Can you give examples of borders in Africa that are literally lines of latitude or longitude?

Yes—several are close to that ideal (even if on the ground there are small deviations due to survey errors or later adjustments): - Egypt–Sudan: The “22nd parallel north” is a classic example, though there’s the Hala’ib Triangle dispute involving different administrative and political claims. - Libya–Chad: The border includes segments that are essentially straight lines determined by colonial-era agreements. - Algeria–Mali and Algeria–Niger: Long straight segments in the Sahara, largely reflecting colonial-era boundary-making where population density is low and physical markers are sparse. - Namibia’s Caprivi Strip (now Zambezi Region): Not a latitude/longitude line exactly, but a striking geometric corridor reflecting German and British colonial negotiations to access the Zambezi River (and, indirectly, hoped-for trade routes). - Sudan–South Sudan: Not purely geometric, but parts of the line reflect administrative boundaries drawn during colonial governance, with disputes (e.g., Abyei) tied to how those lines were defined. Also, the Western Sahara/Morocco/Mauritania area has straight segments influenced by Spanish and French colonial boundaries.
The Sahara seems like it would naturally lead to straight lines because there aren’t rivers and mountains. Is that part of it?

Definitely. Physical geography matters. In deserts, long-distance travel is possible in many directions; permanent settlement is sparse; rivers are rare; and “natural” dividing lines (watersheds, ridges) may be subtle or irrelevant to human movement. So even if borders had emerged organically there, they might still look straighter than borders in mountainous regions. But the key is that many Saharan borders were decided by external powers using geometric tools, and the desert offered few local constraints or objections. In contrast, where African borders run through more densely populated, agriculturally productive zones—West Africa’s coastal belt, the Great Lakes region, the Ethiopian highlands—you see more border complexity and more reliance on rivers and lakes.
What about Europe’s straight borders? You said there are some. Which are the clearest?

A few prominent European cases where borders are notably geometric or “artificial”: - The Portugal–Spain border is actually old and often follows rivers, but there are stretches that are quite straight, reflecting historical treaties and demarcations. - The border between Norway and Sweden includes long segments that are not purely natural-feature-based, though Scandinavia has large forested areas where the line is more about negotiated demarcation than a single obvious river. - The borders created or adjusted after WWII: parts of the Poland–Germany border follow the Oder–Neisse rivers (a natural feature), but other parts of Eastern Europe’s borders—especially where they were drawn to match population transfers—can be more “line-like” or simplified. - Belgium–Netherlands: not straight, but it’s a great example of non-natural borders: enclaves like Baarle-Hertog/Baarle-Nassau are the opposite of geometric simplicity, arising from medieval property and feudal arrangements. - The Italy–Austria and Switzerland–Italy borders often follow high ridges (watersheds), but there are also straight-ish segments resulting from treaties that used passes or points as references. So Europe isn’t “natural borders everywhere”; it’s more that many major frontiers coincide with prominent terrain, and many were refined over time.
Could we quantify “straightness” somehow? Or is it just a visual impression?

You can quantify it. Researchers have tried metrics like: - Fractal dimension / sinuosity: Compare the length of the border to the length of a straight line between its endpoints, or compute fractal properties at multiple scales. More meandering borders have higher sinuosity. - Segment analysis: Break a border into line segments and measure the proportion that are approximately straight over long distances, or the frequency of sharp angle changes. - Correlation with graticules: Measure how much of a border aligns with meridians/parallels (north-south/east-west), which would indicate coordinate-based delimitation. - Terrain-following index: Compare the border path to elevation ridgelines (watersheds) or river networks; how often does it coincide with those features? If you apply these, Africa tends to show a higher fraction of long straight segments and graticule-aligned boundaries than Europe, but the picture varies by subregion.
I’ve heard the term “watershed boundaries.” Why are watersheds considered good borders?

A watershed boundary is the ridge line separating drainage basins: rainfall on one side flows into one river system, on the other side into another. Watersheds can be attractive borders because: - They often coincide with high ground, which historically is defensible and harder to cross with armies. - They can be stable over time compared to river channels (rivers can shift course, causing disputes). - They’re conceptually clear in treaty language (“the divide between Basin A and Basin B”). However, watersheds can be hard to demarcate precisely without good topographic surveys, especially in flat terrain. Also, people don’t necessarily organize their lives around drainage divides; trade routes may cross them. So they’re not inherently “better,” just sometimes convenient.
Rivers can shift? That sounds like it would cause border disputes. How do countries handle that?

Yes—river borders can be legally tricky because rivers meander, avulse (sudden course changes), and create new channels. International law often distinguishes: - Accretion: gradual, natural changes in the river course. Borders may move with the river if the treaty defines the border as the river’s thalweg (the deepest channel) or midline. - Avulsion: sudden changes, like a flood cutting a new channel. Borders often remain in the old channel if the change is abrupt. Treaties and court decisions specify whether the border follows the thalweg, the mid-channel, a bank, or fixed coordinates. That’s why some modern borders along rivers are defined by coordinates or by a surveyed line, even if the river moves.
So if rivers are complicated, why did Europe still use them so much?

Because despite complications, rivers are: - Highly visible and locally meaningful. - Often major transport routes and economic corridors, making them politically salient. - Relatively easy to identify without sophisticated surveying. Historically, before precise mapping, saying “the border is the Rhine” is practical. The complications become more important when states develop strong bureaucracies and legal systems and when small changes matter. Then you see treaties clarifying thalweg rules, joint commissions, and detailed surveys. In Africa, colonial negotiators sometimes did use rivers too—Niger River segments, Congo River segments, Zambezi, Senegal River, etc.—but in many interior/desert areas, rivers weren’t available or weren’t well mapped, and the political logic pushed toward geometric lines.
How much did map quality matter? Like, were they drawing borders on inaccurate maps?

Map quality mattered a lot. In the late 19th century, coastal Africa was better charted than the interior. Explorers, missionaries, and expeditions produced maps of major rivers and lakes, but the precise locations of features could be wrong by tens or even hundreds of kilometers. That led to two kinds of issues: - “Paper borders” that didn’t match geography: A treaty might specify a river confluence that was mislocated, or a lake shoreline that was drawn incorrectly. - Later boundary commissions: As colonial administrations expanded, they had to survey and physically demarcate boundaries. Sometimes they discovered the treaty description was ambiguous or based on erroneous geography, forcing renegotiation or pragmatic adjustments. Straight lines based on coordinates reduced some ambiguity, but only if the coordinates were properly surveyed and if both sides agreed on the datum and measurement methods—another source of error historically.
Datum? That seems like a modern GIS thing.

It’s very relevant historically too, though the terminology evolved. A geodetic datum is a reference model for the Earth’s shape and a coordinate system. Different datums place the same “latitude/longitude” slightly differently on the ground because they assume different ellipsoids and reference points. In the era of colonial boundary surveys, various national surveying systems used different reference ellipsoids and triangulation networks. If a treaty said “the 10th meridian east,” you still needed to know how that meridian was realized on the ground, and different survey teams might not align perfectly. Modern GPS and global datums (like WGS84) reduce these discrepancies, but older boundary pillars and maps can reflect earlier systems. Some border oddities and micro-disputes come from such technical mismatches layered onto political disagreements.
Are there places where African borders follow mountains and watersheds like in Europe?

Yes. A few examples: - The border between Uganda and the Democratic Republic of the Congo includes the Rwenzori Mountains area and follows parts of the Albertine Rift system. - The Ethiopia–Eritrea border involves highland terrain; disputes there were tied to colonial treaties and local administration, but the terrain is mountainous. - Morocco–Algeria includes parts of the Atlas region (though the main dispute is political). - South Africa–Lesotho: Lesotho is a mountainous enclave; the boundary reflects historical and colonial-era delineations but is not a simple straight line. - Rwanda–DRC and Uganda–DRC include Lake Kivu and other Great Lakes features. Also, many borders in southern Africa follow rivers: the Orange River (South Africa–Namibia), the Limpopo (parts of South Africa’s borders), the Zambezi (Zambia–Zimbabwe and others), etc.
If colonial powers drew borders, why didn’t independent African countries redraw them after independence?

Several reasons, often summarized as the “uti possidetis” principle (roughly: “as you possess, so you may possess”), meaning new states keep the administrative boundaries they inherited to avoid endless conflicts. After independence: - Redrawing borders would have opened a huge set of competing claims, likely triggering wars. - Many states were fragile; maintaining recognized boundaries helped gain international legitimacy and UN recognition. - The Organization of African Unity (OAU, predecessor to the African Union) endorsed respecting inherited borders in 1964 to reduce interstate conflict. This didn’t eliminate disputes—there have been many boundary conflicts—but it created a norm that stabilizes the state system. In a sense, the cost of “fixing” colonial borders was judged higher than the cost of living with them.
But Europe redrew borders a lot in the 20th century.

Europe did, but under conditions that were extraordinarily violent and disruptive—two world wars, forced population transfers, the collapse of empires, and later the dissolution of the Soviet bloc and Yugoslavia. Europe’s border changes often came with massive human displacement. In Africa, post-independence leaders and regional organizations largely tried to avoid that kind of continental-scale border revision. Some changes occurred (e.g., Eritrea’s independence from Ethiopia; South Sudan from Sudan), but those were separations rather than wholesale redrawing of many borders at once.
You mentioned ethnic and linguistic groups being split. Is that mainly because of straight lines?

Straight lines can contribute, but the deeper cause is that borders were often drawn with limited concern for existing political and cultural landscapes. Even a river boundary can split a group if the group lives on both banks. That said, geometric borders drawn through interior regions increase the probability of slicing through complex settlement patterns because they’re less likely to coincide with any social frontier. In West Africa, for instance, colonial borders cut across precolonial states and trade networks. In the Horn of Africa, borders intersected pastoral migration routes. But it’s not as simple as “natural borders align with peoples.” Europe’s “natural” borders also cut across languages and identities (e.g., Basque areas across the Pyrenees; German- and French-speaking communities along the Rhine area historically; Slavic groups across the Carpathians). The difference is that Europe’s borders and identities co-evolved over longer periods with repeated renegotiation—often violent.
Could you walk me through a specific border and how it got its shape?

One illustrative case is the Egypt–Sudan border and the Hala’ib Triangle. - In 1899, under British influence, the political boundary between Egypt and Sudan was set at the 22nd parallel north (a clean geometric line). - In 1902, a separate administrative boundary was drawn for practical governance, placing the Hala’ib Triangle under Sudanese administration and a small area (Bir Tawil) under Egyptian administration, based on tribal connections and access. After independence, Egypt and Sudan disagreed on which line is the “true” international boundary. Egypt claims the 22nd parallel (giving it Hala’ib), while Sudan claims the 1902 administrative line. The result is a disputed triangle and an odd unclaimed area (Bir Tawil) because each country’s claim implies the other area belongs to the other state. This shows how a geometric line can be politically neat but socially awkward, and how administrative adjustments can later become the basis for sovereignty claims.
That’s a great example. Another one: what’s the story with the Gambia being that narrow strip?

The Gambia’s shape reflects colonial competition along the Gambia River between Britain and France. Britain established control along the river to secure trade access inland. France controlled the surrounding territory (Senegal). The Gambia became a narrow corridor centered on the navigable river, with the boundary roughly following a fixed distance from the river in places (historically described in terms like “a certain distance from the riverbanks”), later formalized by treaties. So it’s not a latitude/longitude straight line; it’s a “river corridor state” whose shape is dictated by colonial-era control of a trade artery. It looks artificial because it’s an enclave-like strip within Senegal, but it’s anchored to a real geographic feature—the river.
How about the Caprivi Strip you mentioned. Why is it that weird finger?

The Caprivi Strip (Namibia’s Zambezi Region) was part of German South West Africa. Germany negotiated with Britain in the 1890 Heligoland–Zanzibar Treaty, which among other things gave Germany a narrow corridor to the Zambezi River. Germany wanted access to the Zambezi as a potential route to the east, imagining it could connect to German East Africa. In practice, the Zambezi isn’t a smooth transport corridor there due to Victoria Falls and rapids. But the corridor remained, producing that distinctive panhandle. It’s a good example of a border created by strategic imagination and map-based negotiation, not by local settlement patterns or practical geography.
So strategic goals mattered as much as geography?

Very much. Borders are often the residue of strategic bargaining: access to rivers, ports, trade routes, minerals, and buffer zones. Geography shapes what’s valuable (ports, fertile land, chokepoints), but the line itself reflects political decisions. In Africa’s partition, European powers sometimes prioritized: - Access to the coast and navigable rivers. - Control of resource zones (later in the colonial era, more explicitly). - Linking colonies across the continent (e.g., British “Cape to Cairo” vision, though never fully realized as a single continuous dominion). - Preventing rivals from reaching certain areas. Where such objectives could be achieved by drawing a straight line, they often were.
I’m curious about “spheres of influence.” Were those like modern exclusive economic zones?

They’re different. A sphere of influence in the colonial context was a diplomatic claim that a power had priority rights to establish control or negotiate treaties with local rulers in a region, even before fully occupying it. It was a way to prevent rival colonial powers from moving in. It’s unlike an EEZ, which is a legal maritime zone defined by international law (UNCLOS) granting resource rights but not full sovereignty. Colonial spheres were more informal at first, then hardened into formal colonies or protectorates, and their boundaries were often negotiated with other powers—leading to those geometric partitions.
If we compare Africa to the Middle East, I also see straight lines there. Is it the same reason?

Similar dynamics: late imperial partition, map-based treaties, and external powers drawing boundaries with limited local input. In the Middle East, the end of the Ottoman Empire and European mandates after WWI produced several straight or simplified borders (e.g., parts of Iraq–Jordan–Saudi Arabia). But each region has its own history. In Africa, the Scramble period is central; in the Middle East, Ottoman administrative divisions, wartime agreements, and mandate arrangements played big roles. The common thread is relatively rapid boundary-making using modern cartography and diplomacy.
Are straight borders always colonial? Or can they arise “naturally” too?

They can arise without colonialism, though colonialism is a major driver globally. Non-colonial reasons for straight borders include: - Deliberate neutral zones or demilitarized boundaries agreed between adjacent states to simplify disputes. - Land division based on cadastral surveys (property grids) that later become political boundaries (more common in settler societies). - Post-war settlements that draw lines to separate forces or administrative zones (armistice lines can be straight). - Partition agreements where a line is chosen for simplicity or to match a demographic estimate (though demographic lines rarely are straight on the ground). But historically, very long straight international borders are strongly associated with frontier partitioning where one or both sides had limited presence and used map geometry as a tool.
Is there a way to see which African borders were negotiated by which colonial powers?

Yes. You can trace many borders to specific treaties and colonial administrations: - French West Africa (AOF) and French Equatorial Africa (AEF) boundaries often underlie modern borders among francophone states. - British colonies/protectorates (Nigeria, Gold Coast, Kenya, Uganda, etc.) and their administrative boundaries contributed to today’s lines. - German colonies (before WWI) influenced borders in Namibia, Tanzania, Cameroon, Togo, Rwanda/Burundi (later mandates). - Belgian Congo’s boundaries reflect treaties with France, Britain, Portugal, and Germany. - Portuguese Angola and Mozambique had borders negotiated with Britain, Germany, and others. For a technical approach, historical atlases and treaty collections are key. Many national boundary commissions also publish histories. Modern GIS datasets sometimes include “border provenance” notes, but those can be uneven.
Let’s talk about Europe again. Are European borders really “natural,” or is that also a myth?

It’s partly a myth. “Natural borders” is often a political narrative used to justify claims (e.g., “our natural frontier is the river/mountain range”). In practice: - Many European borders follow natural features because they’re convenient markers and defense lines. - But many do not: they follow old feudal boundaries, language frontiers, lines of control after wars, and compromises. - Even “natural” borders are selective: a mountain range might be crossed by a border rather than followed, depending on history. So the contrast is not “Europe natural, Africa artificial.” It’s more “Europe’s borders are older and more path-dependent, while many African borders were fixed quickly in a specific historical moment using modern cartographic tools.”
You mentioned language frontiers. Do those align with geography?

Sometimes. Geography influences settlement and interaction, which influences language spread: - Mountains can isolate valleys, preserving dialects. - Rivers can be corridors (spreading languages) or barriers (separating communities), depending on navigability and bridging. - Plains facilitate movement and mixing. But language boundaries are rarely as crisp as political borders. They’re often gradient zones. Political borders can harden language differences over time through schooling, media, and administration, making a once-gradual transition more abrupt. In Europe, centuries of state-building often reinforced linguistic borders (e.g., standardized national languages), though multilingual states and border regions remain common.
How did surveying actually work on the ground? If a treaty says “straight line from point A to point B,” how do you mark it?

Historically, boundary demarcation involved: - Identifying or creating reference points (often marked by pillars/cairns). - Triangulation: surveyors measure angles between points using theodolites and establish a network of triangles to compute positions. - Astronomical observations: to determine latitude/longitude before satellite systems, surveyors used stars and timekeeping (chronometers). - Cutting “boundary lines” through vegetation in forested regions, creating cleared corridors. - Producing boundary maps and written descriptions, sometimes with coordinates for pillars. In remote areas, the actual demarcated line could deviate from the ideal due to terrain, logistics, or disagreements. Some boundaries were never fully demarcated during colonial times and were only clarified later.
Does that mean some straight borders aren’t actually straight on the ground?

Correct. On a small scale, a “straight” border on a map can be a series of short segments between pillars, with slight deviations due to measurement errors and practical placement. On large-scale maps it looks straight; on the ground it might zigzag subtly. Also, map projections can distort appearance. A line of constant latitude is “straight” on some projections and curved on others. But many African borders are indeed defined as geodesic lines or parallels/meridians, which can be represented differently depending on the projection.
Projection—so when I look at a Mercator map, straight lines might be misleading?

Exactly. On a Mercator projection: - Lines of constant bearing (rhumb lines) are straight. - Meridians and parallels are straight too, but spacing changes with latitude. On other projections: - Great circles (shortest paths on the globe) may appear curved. - Meridians converge toward the poles; some projections show them as curved. So a border defined by “the 22nd parallel” will appear as a straight horizontal line on many common projections, but if you use certain projections or view it on a globe, it’s a circle around Earth. That said, the “geometric” impression of many African borders is robust across projections because many are aligned to cardinal directions and have long linear segments.
Is there an example where a border was intended to follow a river but ended up straight because the river was mis-mapped?

There are cases where treaties referenced rivers whose courses or identities were unclear, leading to later straight-line compromises or disputes. One pattern is: - Treaty specifies “the river X to its source, then a line to point Y.” - Later, surveyors disagree on which tributary is the “true” river X or where the “source” is. - A compromise line is drawn, sometimes straight, to connect agreed points. A famous non-African example is the Alaska boundary dispute (US/Canada), but in Africa similar issues happened with tributaries in West and Central Africa. The specifics vary; often the final demarcation uses a mix of river segments and straight connectors.
If Africa had more time, would its borders become less straight?

Possibly, but not inevitably. Borders can become more complex through: - Negotiated adjustments (land swaps, minor corrections). - Secessions and new states. - Resolution of disputes with detailed demarcation that follows local realities. However, there’s also a strong global norm of border stability. Many states prefer not to tinker with borders because it risks opening broader claims. So even over long periods, inherited straight borders can persist. Europe’s borders became less geometric in part because they were never “designed” all at once; they accreted through many small changes. Africa’s borders were “locked in” relatively early in the postcolonial era by international norms.
Are there any African regions where borders are especially non-straight?

The Great Lakes region stands out: Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi, DR Congo, Tanzania, Kenya—borders there interact with lakes (Victoria, Tanganyika, Kivu, Albert, Edward) and rift geography, producing more complex shapes. West Africa’s coastal region also has intricate borders due to historical coastal forts, trade zones, and dense precolonial polities. The Guinea–Liberia–Sierra Leone area, for example, is less geometric than the Saharan boundaries. Southern Africa includes many river-based boundaries and some historically negotiated lines around enclaves and protectorates (Lesotho, Eswatini), adding complexity.
How do lakes work as borders? Is it the shoreline or a midline?

It depends on treaties and customary practice: - Some borders follow the midline (equidistant line) through the lake. - Some follow the thalweg in navigable channels (more relevant in rivers than lakes). - Some assign the entire lake to one state (rarer, but exists in some cases). - Some define borders by coordinates or by specific islands. Lakes can be tricky because water levels change and islands can appear/disappear. Also, fisheries and resources matter. Lake Victoria is shared; Lake Malawi/Nyasa has a notable dispute between Malawi and Tanzania about whether the border runs down the midline or along one shore based on colonial-era wording.
Speaking of disputes, are straight borders more likely to cause conflicts?

Not necessarily by geometry alone. Conflict risk correlates more with: - Resource distribution (oil, minerals, water). - Strategic value (ports, chokepoints). - Ethno-political divisions and state capacity. - Ambiguity in treaty language and demarcation. A straight border can be perfectly clear and peaceful if both sides accept it and it doesn’t cut through contested resource zones. Conversely, a river border can be disputed if the river shifts or if the treaty is ambiguous. That said, borders that ignore local social and economic networks can create long-term grievances, which may contribute to internal conflict even if interstate conflict is avoided.
Can we compare Africa’s borders to the Americas? The US-Canada border is famously straight in places.

The Americas are a strong comparison. The US–Canada border includes: - The 49th parallel for long stretches (very geometric). - The Great Lakes and river segments (more natural-feature-based). This reflects treaties between two expanding states/colonial powers, negotiated with coordinate lines where convenient and natural features where salient. Many Latin American borders are also products of colonial administrative divisions and later wars, and some are quite straight (especially in sparsely populated areas), while others follow watersheds in the Andes (Chile–Argentina uses watershed principles in many sections but has disputes because watersheds and highest peaks don’t always coincide). So Africa isn’t unique in having straight borders; it’s just that the concentration of late, externally negotiated partitions across a whole continent makes the pattern more visually striking.
You mentioned the Andes dispute—why wouldn’t the watershed match the highest peaks?

In mountainous terrain, people sometimes assume “the highest ridge” is the watershed divide, but geology and glaciation can create cases where: - The highest peaks are offset from the drainage divide. - Water flows through passes or around massifs in non-intuitive ways. - Glacial meltwater patterns change. Treaties might say “the highest peaks” or “the watershed,” and if those differ, you get ambiguity. In the Chile–Argentina case, disagreements arose in Patagonia where the “divortium aquarum” (watershed) and the main cordillera peaks aren’t always aligned. That required arbitration. This illustrates that even “natural” borders can be technically complex.
Back to Africa—were there any attempts to draw borders based on ethnic groups?

There were occasional attempts or at least discussions, but systematically drawing borders to match ethnic distributions would have been extremely difficult: - Ethnic identities are not fixed, and distributions are intermingled. - Many groups are dispersed, and boundaries are fuzzy. - Colonial ethnography often simplified or mischaracterized identities. - Political units (kingdoms, emirates, city-states) didn’t map neatly onto “tribes” as Europeans imagined. Moreover, colonial powers were primarily concerned with their own rivalry and administrative control, not creating ethnically coherent states. Some boundaries incidentally aligned with certain groups or precolonial polities, but many did not.
I want a more technical cartographic explanation. Why are lines of latitude/longitude so tempting in treaties?

Because they’re: - Universal: In principle, any surveyor can locate them using astronomical observations. - Unambiguous in text: “The boundary follows the 15°E meridian from latitude A to latitude B” is concise and clear. - Independent of local feature naming: Rivers can have multiple names; “source” can be disputed; a “mountain” might have no agreed name. Coordinates avoid that. - Scalable: You can define long boundaries without listing many local landmarks. The downside is that implementing such a line requires accurate geodesy and agreement on reference systems. But compared to negotiating a boundary through a mosaic of local jurisdictions, coordinate lines were diplomatically efficient.
Did local African rulers have any input at all?

It varied. In many cases, colonial powers signed treaties with local rulers—often under unequal conditions—granting protectorate status or trade rights, which were then used to justify claims to European rivals. But the inter-imperial boundaries between colonial possessions were usually negotiated between European powers, not by local polities. Some local resistance, alliances, and wars influenced where colonial control could be asserted, which indirectly affected boundaries. In some regions, strong states (e.g., Ethiopia) maintained sovereignty and thus had more agency in boundary negotiations, though still under pressure from imperial powers.
Ethiopia’s borders don’t look as straight. Is that because it wasn’t colonized?

Ethiopia is a partial exception: it maintained independence (aside from the Italian occupation in the 1930s) and negotiated borders with colonial neighbors (Italian Eritrea, British Somaliland/Kenya/Sudan, French Djibouti). Its borders reflect a mix of historical expansion, treaties, and geography—so they’re less dominated by long coordinate lines than some Saharan boundaries. But Ethiopia’s borders were still influenced by colonial-era treaties and modern diplomacy, and there are straight segments too. The broader point is that where local state formation and military power shaped boundaries, you tend to get more historically contingent, less purely geometric lines.
What about Somalia? That’s an example where ethnic Somalis are split across borders, right?

Yes. Somali-inhabited regions extend beyond Somalia’s current borders into: - Ethiopia (Ogaden region) - Kenya (Northeastern Province) - Djibouti (Somali and Afar populations) These splits reflect colonial boundaries among Britain, Italy, France, and Ethiopia. The result contributed to irredentist politics after independence (e.g., Somalia’s pursuit of “Greater Somalia” in the 1960s–70s), including conflict with Ethiopia and Kenya. This is a case where borders didn’t match a strong ethnolinguistic identity, creating long-term tensions.
Europe also has split ethnic groups. Why didn’t that cause the same kind of border revisionism?

Europe did have a lot of revisionism—especially in the first half of the 20th century. Many conflicts were explicitly about ethnic minorities and “national self-determination.” The difference is partly timing and global context: - After WWII, Europe’s borders were stabilized through a mix of enforced settlements and population transfers (often brutal), plus the Cold War order. - European integration later reduced the salience of borders for trade and movement (at least within the EU/Schengen), lowering incentives for revisionism. - In Africa, new states emerged into a world where border stability was strongly favored by international institutions, and where changing borders risked widespread cascading conflicts. So Europe’s relative stability today is the result of a very turbulent earlier period and later institutional integration, not a lack of ethnic-border mismatch.
How did colonial powers decide which meridian or parallel to use? Was it arbitrary?

Often it was pragmatic and somewhat arbitrary: - Use a round-number parallel/meridian because it’s easy to describe. - Use a line that roughly splits claimed spheres of influence without needing detailed local mapping. - Use a line connecting known points (a coast point, a river confluence, a lake) with straight segments. - Sometimes use a line to avoid giving a rival access to a river basin or route. In some cases, the chosen line was meant to approximate a divide between areas of influence derived from earlier treaties with local rulers. But because those local treaties were themselves vague and opportunistic, the resulting coordinate line could be quite arbitrary relative to local realities.
Does the straightness correlate with colonial power? Like did Britain draw straighter lines than France?

It’s hard to generalize cleanly because border shapes reflect bilateral negotiations. Many boundaries were between British and French possessions, so attributing the geometry to one power is misleading. That said, French colonial administration often emphasized large territorial units and sometimes preferred clear, administratively manageable boundaries; British practice varied between direct and indirect rule. But the geometry often came from the negotiation context—especially where both sides wanted a quick, verifiable delimitation in sparsely mapped areas. A more useful correlation is with terrain and population density: deserts and thinly populated interiors tend to have straighter borders regardless of which colonial powers were involved.
If I zoom in on a map, I see some borders that are straight for a while and then suddenly jog. What causes those jogs?

Several causes: - Treaty-defined turning points: A boundary might follow a parallel to a certain meridian, then turn to follow a river, then turn again. - Connecting segments: Straight “connectors” are used to link natural features (river to river, lake to ridge). - Survey corrections: Demarcation commissions sometimes adjusted a line to pass through a specific landmark or to correct earlier mapping errors, creating small kinks. - Tripoints: Where three colonies met, compromises could produce jogs so each side got or avoided certain features. In cadastral contexts (more common in North America), jogs can reflect township grids. In Africa, they more often reflect treaty geometry and later demarcation pragmatics.
Tell me about tripoints. How do they get decided?

A tripoint is where three countries meet. Deciding it can be tricky because: - Two borders may have been negotiated separately at different times. - Treaties may not align perfectly or may reference different features. - Survey errors accumulate. Typically, tripoints are resolved by trilateral agreements and joint surveys. They may be marked with a monument. Sometimes tripoints remain undefined or disputed, especially in remote areas, which can complicate resource management and security.
Is there an example of a famous African tripoint issue?

One example of tripoint complexity is in the Great Lakes region and the Nile basin, where multiple borders meet near lakes and rivers and where colonial-era treaties, mandates, and later independence created layered legal claims. Another is in the Sahel where Mali, Niger, Burkina Faso meet—there have been localized disputes and security issues, though those are also tied to governance and insurgency, not just the tripoint itself. In many cases, the technical tripoint demarcation is less publicly famous than the broader political conflicts in the region, but boundary commissions deal with these details.
You’ve talked a lot about interstate borders. What about internal administrative boundaries—do they show the same straightness?

Often, yes—especially where administrative boundaries were drawn during colonial times for management convenience. Internal boundaries can be even more geometric because they’re less politically sensitive internationally and can be drawn to balance administrative workload rather than reflect geography. However, internal boundaries also sometimes follow ethnic regions, historical kingdoms, or ecological zones, depending on the state’s governance approach. Postcolonial states have redrawn internal regions for political reasons (power balancing, decentralization), producing a mix of geometric and organic shapes.
So the visual impression is partly about scale too?

Exactly. At continental scale, long straight segments stand out. At local scale, even “straight” borders can have detailed demarcation features and minor deviations. Conversely, a border that looks curvy at continental scale might be composed of many short straight segments following a river’s general course or a ridge line. Map generalization also matters: small-scale maps smooth complex lines, and the level of detail in the dataset influences how “natural” a border looks.
Could we talk about how national borders relate to tectonic features? Like rift valleys in Africa?

Tectonic features can influence borders indirectly because they shape topography, lakes, and river systems. The East African Rift, for instance, created major lakes (Tanganyika, Malawi, Turkana) and highlands that became natural reference features. Borders around these lakes and along escarpments sometimes follow the resulting geography. But borders rarely follow tectonic lines as such; they follow the surface features humans can use—lakes, ridges, valleys. Where rift lakes provide clear markers, they become boundaries; where the rift is a broad zone, borders may cut across it.
If European borders were older, why didn’t they also use latitude/longitude when those became available?

They sometimes did, but by the time coordinate-based mapping became widespread, many European borders were already established and politically entrenched. Changing them to coordinate lines would have been unnecessary and provocative. Also, European borders often ran through densely settled areas where using a coordinate line would slice through towns, farms, and infrastructure, creating immediate governance problems. In contrast, many colonial-era coordinate lines were drawn across sparsely settled regions (from the colonizers’ perspective), reducing immediate administrative friction—though not eliminating long-term issues.
How did the concept of sovereignty differ? Did colonial borders even mean much on the ground?

Early on, many colonial borders were more diplomatic than real. In the late 19th century, “effective occupation” was a criterion, but in practice control was uneven. Borders in remote areas might not have been patrolled; local communities moved across them; and colonial authority might be limited to a few posts. Over time, as administrations expanded, borders became more meaningful: customs, policing, taxation, and identity documents made them real in daily life. After independence, states inherited these boundaries and gradually increased their practical significance, though permeability remains high in many regions due to trade and kinship networks.
That raises a question: do straight borders correlate with porous borders?

Not directly. Porosity depends on governance capacity, terrain, and economic networks. A straight border in a desert can be hard to patrol and thus porous. A straight border through a fenced or heavily monitored area can be less porous. A river border might be porous if there are many crossing points and weak enforcement. In practice, many African borders—straight or not—are porous because of long-standing cross-border trade, pastoral mobility, and limited state presence in borderlands. But geometry isn’t the main determinant.
Some people say Africa’s borders are “unnatural.” Is that a useful term?

It’s rhetorically powerful but analytically slippery. All borders are human constructs; calling some “unnatural” implies there’s a “natural” way to divide political space, which is rarely true. A more precise critique is: - Many borders were drawn without local consent and with limited knowledge. - They often split communities and ignore economic/ecological regions. - They were imposed rapidly and then frozen. Those are concrete claims you can evaluate historically. But “unnatural” can obscure the fact that even borders following rivers or mountains can be politically arbitrary and socially disruptive.
Can you explain how precolonial political boundaries worked in Africa? Were they fixed lines?

Often they were not fixed lines in the modern sense. Many precolonial polities had: - Core areas of strong control (capitals, major towns, agricultural zones). - Frontier zones with weaker control, tribute relationships, or shifting alliances. - Overlapping influence: multiple authorities might claim rights (trade, tribute, religious authority) over the same area. So boundaries were frequently zones rather than lines. That’s also true historically in Europe (marches, borderlands). Modern cartographic borders turn fuzzy frontiers into precise lines, which can create conflicts when social reality is more fluid.
Interesting. So colonialism didn’t just draw lines; it changed the whole idea of borders.

Exactly. Colonial administration and modern statehood introduced: - Linear boundaries surveyed and mapped. - Territorial sovereignty as exclusive control within those lines. - Standardized taxation, law, and policing tied to territory. This shift from frontier zones to fixed lines happened globally with the rise of the modern state system, but in Africa it was compressed into a shorter period and imposed through external power, making the discontinuity more visible.
Are there any cases where African borders were adjusted peacefully after independence?

Yes, though they’re less prominent than disputes. Countries have negotiated minor adjustments and clarified demarcations through joint commissions. Examples include: - Boundary demarcation agreements in southern Africa where river borders required clarification. - Land swaps or local adjustments to align with villages or administrative convenience. - International Court of Justice (ICJ) cases resolved peacefully, like Cameroon–Nigeria over the Bakassi Peninsula (though implementation had tensions, the legal resolution was through ICJ and subsequent agreements). These show that borders can be managed through legal and diplomatic mechanisms, not only conflict.
Bakassi—was that about a river border or coastline?

The Bakassi Peninsula dispute between Cameroon and Nigeria involved colonial-era treaties and the interpretation of boundaries around the Cross River estuary and coastal areas. It wasn’t a simple straight-line issue; it involved river channels, maritime access, and colonial legal documents (including agreements between Britain and Germany). It highlights how complex border disputes can be even when geometry isn’t the main factor.
Maritime boundaries—do those tend to be straight?

Maritime boundaries are often defined by: - Equidistance/median lines (geometrically derived). - Lines connecting coordinate points. - Adjustments for special circumstances (coastline shape, islands, proportionality). So they can look quite geometric, especially in open seas. But they can also be complex due to islands and irregular coastlines. Modern maritime delimitation is heavily technical and legal, using geodesy and UNCLOS principles, so “straight segments between coordinates” is common.
If we apply that back to Africa, could we say the borders are “modern” rather than “colonial”?

In a technical sense, many borders are modern because they’re defined with modern cartographic concepts and later embedded in the modern international legal order. But historically, the impetus and initial design were colonial. So “modern-imperial” might be closer: modern tools used for imperial partition, then inherited by modern states. It’s also important that many borders have been reaffirmed and managed by independent African states for decades now, so they’re not only colonial artifacts; they’re also part of contemporary political geography.
How do textbooks typically oversimplify this?

Common oversimplifications: - “Berlin Conference drew all borders”: It didn’t; many borders came later through bilateral treaties and on-the-ground processes. - “Europe has natural borders, Africa has artificial borders”: Both regions have mixtures; the key differences are timing, process, and scale. - “Straight borders cause all conflicts”: Conflicts have many causes; borders interact with them but aren’t sole drivers. - “Ethnic borders would solve everything”: Ethnic distributions are complex; drawing borders on that basis could create new minorities and conflicts. A more accurate account emphasizes the interplay of cartography, imperial diplomacy, geography, and postcolonial norms.
What would a counterexample look like—an African border that’s old and not colonial?

Most current African international borders were formalized during the colonial period or in its aftermath. However, there are older boundaries that influenced modern borders: - Morocco’s historical frontiers and claims predate the Scramble, though modern borders were shaped by French/Spanish protectorates. - Ethiopia’s historical boundaries and expansions predate European colonization, though modern borders were treaty-defined with colonial neighbors. - Egypt’s boundaries have deep historical roots, but modern international borders (especially with Sudan, Libya, Israel) were shaped by colonial and post-Ottoman arrangements. So truly “non-colonial” borders are limited, but precolonial political geography influenced some modern lines.
You mentioned Israel—Sinai border is pretty straight too, right?

The Egypt–Israel border (and historically Egypt–Ottoman/Palestine boundary) includes relatively straight segments, reflecting early 20th-century delimitation between British-controlled Egypt and the Ottoman Empire, later inherited. It’s another example of modern boundary-making using straight segments in desert terrain, where natural markers are sparse and the political need was clarity.
So deserts plus modern diplomacy equals straight lines.

That’s a good heuristic, with caveats. Add: - Sparse settlement (from the perspective of state administration). - Limited detailed mapping at the time of negotiation. - Inter-state bargaining where simplicity is valuable. Where those conditions hold—Sahara, Arabian Desert, parts of Australia’s internal borders, western North America—you often see straight lines.
Australia’s state borders are very straight. That seems like the clearest comparison.

Yes. Australian state/territory borders are heavily graticule-based (parallels and meridians), reflecting British colonial administration over a vast, sparsely settled continent (again, from the colonizers’ perspective). That’s a strong analogy for parts of Africa, especially the Sahara and interior regions. The difference is that Australia’s internal borders are within one country today, while Africa’s colonial partitions became international borders between sovereign states, raising the stakes.
If we were to redesign borders today using “best practices,” what would those be?

In theory, “best practices” for stable borders might include: - Clarity: unambiguous legal descriptions and precise demarcation. - Local consultation: align boundaries with lived social/economic networks where possible. - Administrative viability: ensure states/regions have access to resources, infrastructure, and coherent governance units. - Conflict sensitivity: avoid splitting key communities or creating isolated enclaves without agreements. - Flexibility mechanisms: cross-border cooperation, shared resource regimes, special autonomous regions. But in practice, redrawing international borders is extremely risky; the best “practice” today is often to keep borders but reduce their divisive effects through regional integration, cross-border institutions, and minority protections.
That sounds like the EU model.

It’s analogous in the sense of reducing the cost of borders—trade, travel, regulatory alignment—without changing sovereignty. Africa has regional economic communities (ECOWAS, EAC, SADC, etc.) that aim to facilitate movement and trade, though implementation varies. De-emphasizing borders can mitigate some problems created by their historical shapes.
So maybe the real question isn’t “why are they straight” but “why were they frozen.”

Exactly. The straightness is a symptom of how they were drawn; the persistence is about postcolonial state formation and international norms. The freezing happened because: - Changing borders invites conflict. - International recognition depends on defined territory. - Regional organizations promoted stability. - Many leaders prioritized consolidating control within inherited boundaries. So the geometry is historically interesting, but the institutional choice to maintain inherited borders is what makes those lines enduring features of political geography.
Let me press on one thing: Europe’s borders sometimes follow rivers, but rivers were also trade routes. Wouldn’t you want to control both sides?

Often, yes—which is why rivers can be contested rather than accepted as borders. But rivers can also be used as shared arteries. Historically: - Some states sought to control both banks for security and taxation. - Others accepted a river border when the river was a defensible line or when neither could dominate the other. - In many cases, treaties granted navigation rights regardless of sovereignty (e.g., international river commissions). So a river border is a compromise: it can separate jurisdictions while still enabling shared use through agreements. In Europe, dense diplomacy and institutions evolved around river navigation (Rhine Commission, Danube Commission). In Africa, some international river basin organizations exist today, but colonial borders often cut across basins without integrated management at the time.
Do African borders cut across river basins more than Europe’s?

Often, yes—especially because many borders were drawn without hydrological considerations. You can see major basins (Nile, Niger, Congo, Zambezi) spanning many countries. Europe also has transboundary basins (Danube spans many states), but Europe has had longer to build cooperative regimes, and some borders do follow watershed divides in mountainous areas. In Africa, basin fragmentation can complicate water management, but it also creates incentives for cooperation through basin authorities—though politics and unequal power can make cooperation difficult.
If borders don’t follow watersheds, does that increase flood risk or water disputes?

It can increase governance complexity. Flood risk is primarily physical (rainfall, land use, infrastructure), but managing it requires coordination upstream and downstream. When a basin is divided among many states, coordination is harder, especially if there are conflicts or capacity gaps. Water disputes can arise over: - Dams and flow regulation. - Irrigation withdrawals. - Pollution. - Navigation rights. But disputes aren’t inevitable; frameworks and treaties can manage them. The Nile Basin is a prominent example of complex multi-state negotiation.
How does the Nile Basin compare to, say, the Rhine in terms of border logic?

The Rhine is a European river that partly forms borders (e.g., France–Germany in sections) and runs through highly institutionalized governance systems with long histories of navigation and pollution control agreements. The Nile does not serve as a single border line in the same way; it’s more a shared resource crossing many internal territories. Borders in the Nile Basin reflect colonial-era partitions and historical polities, not a design to manage the river as a boundary. The governance challenge is basin-wide coordination rather than bilateral border management. So the Rhine illustrates how a river can be both a border and a shared corridor under strong institutions; the Nile illustrates how basin fragmentation can complicate regional water politics.
I think I get it now. But I still wonder: are straight borders inherently “bad” for state formation?

Not inherently. A straight border can be: - Clear and easy to demarcate. - Less likely to generate micro-disputes about shifting channels. - Neutral in the sense of not privileging one side of a river valley. The problems arise when the border: - Cuts across dense populations and networks without mitigation. - Creates awkward access (e.g., isolates a region from its economic hinterland). - Is ambiguous or poorly demarcated. - Intersects with resource hotspots in contested ways. State formation depends more on institutions, legitimacy, economic integration, and governance than on border geometry. Border shape can influence these, but it’s not destiny.
You’ve been careful not to moralize, which is good. Can you summarize the main causal chain in a compact way?

Sure: - Late 19th–early 20th century: European imperial competition accelerates territorial claims in Africa. - Diplomacy and treaties: Colonial powers negotiate boundaries quickly, often from maps, favoring unambiguous coordinate-based lines and simple segments. - Terrain and mapping: In deserts and poorly mapped interiors, natural features are scarce or uncertain; straight lines are easiest. - Demarcation: Survey commissions later mark boundaries, sometimes introducing minor deviations but preserving overall geometry. - Independence and norms: New African states inherit these borders and largely keep them under a norm of territorial integrity to avoid widespread conflict. Europe’s borders, by contrast, are older, evolved through long local contestation, and often anchored in terrain and historical jurisdictions—though not always.
Good. Now, could you list a few more African borders that follow rivers or lakes, just to balance the picture?

Examples include: - Senegal–Mauritania: largely along the Senegal River. - DR Congo–Republic of the Congo: along the Congo River in places (including near Kinshasa–Brazzaville). - Zambia–Zimbabwe: along the Zambezi River (including around Lake Kariba). - South Africa–Namibia: along the Orange River. - Uganda–Tanzania: includes Lake Victoria segments. - Tanzania–DR Congo: includes Lake Tanganyika. - Kenya–Uganda and Kenya–Tanzania: include Lake Victoria segments. - Malawi–Mozambique: includes Lake Malawi/Nyasa shoreline segments (with dispute involving Tanzania on the northeastern side). These show that African borders often do use major hydrological features; the “straight line” story is most pronounced in the Sahara/Sahel and some interior partitions.
Thanks. One last question: if I’m looking at a political map, what are the visual cues that a border was likely drawn by colonial treaty rather than evolving locally?

Visual cues (not foolproof) include: - Very long straight segments, especially aligned north-south or east-west. - Borders that form near-perfect rectangles or trapezoids. - Borders that cut across major ecological zones without following rivers/ridges. - Narrow corridors/panhandles created to reach a river or coast. - Borders that ignore settlement patterns (hard to infer from a political map alone, but you can cross-check with population density maps). To be confident, you’d corroborate with historical records: treaty dates, colonial possession maps, and demarcation commission reports.
That’s exactly what I needed.
What will the next supercontinent look like? Is Pangaea Ultima the leading theory?

Several “next supercontinent” endmembers are actively discussed, and none is universally “the leading” theory because the outcome depends on which subduction systems persist, where new subduction initiates, and how mantle convection reorganizes. The main named scenarios are: - Pangaea Ultima (sometimes also called Pangea Proxima in older popularizations): the Atlantic closes and the Americas collide with Africa–Europe, yielding a supercontinent centered roughly near the equator. - Amasia: the Arctic closes; the Americas drift northward and collide with Eurasia near the North Pole. - Novopangaea: the Pacific closes; the Americas continue moving westward; Eurasia and Africa migrate eastward, producing a supercontinent assembled around the Pacific margins. These are simplified attractors. Real outcomes could be hybrids: partial Atlantic closure plus Arctic closure, or a supercontinent assembled with large interior seas, or a “two-continent” world for a while.
Can you explain what controls whether the Atlantic or Pacific closes? It seems arbitrary in some documentaries.

It’s not arbitrary, but it is contingent. The key control is where long-lived subduction zones exist and where new ones can start. 1) Present-day subduction asymmetry - The Pacific is ringed by subduction zones (the “Ring of Fire”) and is shrinking in area overall because oceanic lithosphere is being consumed at many trenches. - The Atlantic has comparatively little subduction; it’s dominated by the Mid-Atlantic Ridge spreading center. Oceanic lithosphere is being created there, not destroyed, so the Atlantic is generally widening. 2) Subduction initiation For the Atlantic to close, you typically need subduction to initiate along one or both Atlantic margins (e.g., off the Americas or off Africa/Europe). Subduction initiation is hard: it requires a combination of old, dense oceanic lithosphere, pre-existing weaknesses, compressive stresses, and favorable plate boundary forces. Margins that are currently “passive” (no trench) can become active, but it’s rare and tends to occur at inherited zones of weakness or where plate motions change. 3) Slab pull and plate-driving forces - Slab pull from sinking oceanic plates is a major driver. The Pacific has many slabs pulling plates toward trenches. - Ridge push and basal tractions matter too, but slab pull often dominates. 4) Mantle convection reorganizations Large-scale mantle flow can change over tens to hundreds of millions of years. Plumes, large low-shear-velocity provinces (LLSVPs), and slab graveyards can influence where rifting or subduction is favored. So, the Pacific closing is “easier” given current boundary conditions, but supercontinent cycles may involve switching which ocean dominates: an “interior” ocean that formed during breakup might later close (the Atlantic), while an “exterior” ocean (the Pacific remnant of Panthalassa) might persist longer—or vice versa. Different models weight these tendencies differently.
I’ve heard about the “introversion vs extroversion” ideas. How do those map onto these named scenarios?

Those terms are a useful shorthand for how a supercontinent re-assembles relative to the previous supercontinent. - Extroversion: The supercontinent re-assembles by closing the “exterior” ocean that surrounded the previous supercontinent. After Pangaea, the exterior ocean was Panthalassa; today’s Pacific is often treated as its descendant. Extroversion corresponds broadly to a scenario where the Pacific closes and continents gather into a new supercontinent assembled around the former Pacific realm (often aligned with “Novopangaea,” though details differ). - Introversion: The supercontinent re-assembles by closing the “interior” oceans that opened during breakup. After Pangaea, that interior ocean is the Atlantic. Introversion corresponds to “Pangaea Ultima”-style outcomes where the Atlantic closes and the Americas collide with Africa/Europe. - Orthoversion: The next supercontinent forms roughly 90 degrees away from the previous one in terms of mantle convection patterns and the distribution of subduction. This is tied to the idea that the next aggregation tends to occur above a ring of subduction encircling the previous supercontinent, with assembly occurring over a mantle structure that’s rotated relative to the last supercontinent. These aren’t mutually exclusive in practice. You can have partial introversion (Atlantic narrowing in one sector) while the Pacific continues shrinking, and the final geometry might look like a blend.
If we fast-forward 250 million years, what are the most common “map outcomes” people propose? Could you describe them like a geographic sketch?

Sure—keeping in mind that these are conceptual and that exact longitudes/latitudes are highly uncertain. 1) Pangaea Ultima / Atlantic-closure sketch (introversion) - North America moves east/southeast relative to Eurasia–Africa, closing the North Atlantic. Greenland and the North Atlantic basin disappear. - South America swings east, colliding with West Africa; the South Atlantic closes. - Africa plows into Europe more completely than today; the Mediterranean is gone. - The Indian Ocean may shrink as Australia and India continue north and interact with Southeast Asia; East Africa rifting may or may not succeed before collision dynamics change. - Result: a supercontinent roughly straddling the equator, with a large “super-Tethys-like” interior seaway possibly persisting for a time depending on how Asia stitches together. 2) Novopangaea / Pacific-closure sketch (extroversion) - The Americas continue drifting west (or rotate so that their west coasts keep subducting oceanic lithosphere), and the Pacific basin closes. - East Asia and Australia shift northward; Antarctica may move north and dock to Australia or southern Asia. - The Atlantic continues widening; it becomes the dominant global ocean. - Result: a supercontinent assembled where the Pacific used to be, with a huge Atlantic “Pan-Atlantic” ocean surrounding it. 3) Amasia / Arctic-closure sketch - North America and Eurasia converge across the Arctic; the Arctic Ocean closes. - The Atlantic might still exist as a broad ocean, but its northern sector diminishes; the Pacific may also continue shrinking. - Result: a supercontinent centered at high northern latitudes, possibly with a large equatorial ocean belt. 4) Hybrid outcomes - An “Americas–Eurasia” collision in the north (Amasia-like) plus South America–Africa collision in the south (Pangaea Ultima-like). - Or a Pacific closure that’s incomplete, leaving a remnant oceanic seaway while other basins close. If you want, I can pick one scenario and walk continent-by-continent (Africa, Eurasia, North America, South America, Australia, Antarctica, India, Arabia) describing plausible motions and collision zones.
Do that for Pangaea Ultima first, but keep it grounded in what we see today: current plate motions, subduction zones, ridges.

For a Pangaea Ultima-style outcome, you’re essentially asking: what sequence of plausible plate-boundary changes could convert today’s Atlantic-dominated spreading system into a future Atlantic-collisional system? I’ll go region by region, tying it to present tectonics. 1) North America and the North Atlantic Today: - The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is creating new crust between North America and Eurasia. - There is no major subduction along the Atlantic margins (passive margins). - In the north, the plate boundary complexity includes Iceland (ridge + plume), the Azores, and interactions with the Arctic (Gakkel Ridge spreading). For Atlantic closure: - You’d typically need subduction initiation somewhere along the Atlantic. One plausible “seed” is the Gibraltar arc region and the complex subduction/collision system around the Mediterranean. Another is the Caribbean–Scotia arcs, but those are not “Atlantic-margin” trenches in the classic sense. - If subduction initiates along the eastern margin of North America (offshore), the North Atlantic could begin to be consumed. Alternatively, subduction could initiate along the western margin of Europe/Africa, consuming Atlantic lithosphere from that side. Consequence: - Over tens of millions of years, the North Atlantic narrows. Greenland’s position becomes moot as it gets caught in collision, potentially becoming a mountain belt fragment. 2) South America and the South Atlantic Today: - South America is moving west relative to Africa, but the South Atlantic is still spreading at the Mid-Atlantic Ridge segment. - The Andes are driven by subduction of the Nazca Plate beneath South America (Pacific side). For Atlantic closure: - If South America’s motion relative to Africa changes (or if Africa changes), and/or if subduction initiates along one South Atlantic margin, the basin can begin to close. - The Andes subduction system may persist for a long time, but as the geometry of plates changes, the Nazca Plate (or its successors) could be reorganized. A key uncertainty is whether Pacific subduction continues to dominate South American motion or whether new Atlantic-side forces take over. Consequence: - South America collides with West Africa (Brazil–Gulf of Guinea region), building a new orogen comparable in scale to Appalachians–Caledonides–Variscides but potentially larger depending on convergence rates and duration. 3) Africa and Europe (Mediterranean closure) Today: - Africa is converging with Eurasia; the Mediterranean has subduction zones (Hellenic, Calabrian, etc.), collision zones (Alps), and complex microplates (Aegean, Anatolia). - The Red Sea is opening; East Africa Rift is active. In Pangaea Ultima: - The Mediterranean almost certainly closes relatively “soon” (tens of Myr) in most long-term projections because convergence is ongoing. - Africa’s northward push stitches Africa to Europe more completely, eliminating the Mediterranean basin. Complication: - East Africa rifting could, if it fully develops, split Africa into two plates and open a new ocean. But whether that ocean persists depends on later plate reorganizations; it could also be closed in the run-up to supercontinent assembly. 4) Arabia, India, and Asia Today: - Arabia is moving north, closing the Zagros and colliding with Eurasia. - India has already collided with Eurasia; convergence continues, building the Himalaya–Tibet system. - Southeast Asia is a collage of microplates with subduction along the Sunda trench. In Pangaea Ultima: - Arabia becomes fully sutured to Eurasia; the Persian Gulf region becomes an orogenic belt. - India continues to underthrust Asia; over very long times, convergence may slow as buoyant crust resists subduction, but lateral extrusion and plateau growth continue. - Southeast Asia’s subduction zones may shift as Australia moves north. 5) Australia and the Indian Ocean Today: - Australia is moving north toward Southeast Asia; the Indonesian region has active subduction and complex collision. - The Indian Ocean has spreading ridges; parts of it are bounded by subduction (e.g., Sunda). In Pangaea Ultima: - Australia collides with Southeast Asia, thickening crust and building mountain belts across what is now Indonesia and New Guinea. - Depending on how the Indian Ocean ridges evolve, parts of the Indian Ocean might be consumed if subduction zones encroach. If Africa is moving differently, the Indian Ocean could shrink as Africa–Eurasia–Australia interactions intensify. 6) Antarctica and the Southern oceans Today: - Antarctica is mostly surrounded by mid-ocean ridges and transform faults; it’s relatively isolated with the Antarctic Circumpolar Current. In Pangaea Ultima: - Antarctica could remain peripheral or become involved if Australia drags it northward or if South America–Antarctica linkages change. But in many Pangaea Ultima sketches, Antarctica stays attached to the supercontinent margin or docks later. Overall “geographic sketch”: - Imagine a broad equatorial landmass: the Americas welded to Africa–Europe; Asia attached on the east; Australia sutured into Southeast Asia; Antarctica possibly appended to the south. The major ocean becomes a “super-Pacific-like” basin (or a remnant of it) on the opposite side if the Pacific hasn’t fully closed. The fragile point is subduction initiation in the Atlantic. Without it, the Atlantic keeps widening and introversion becomes less likely.
So for Pangaea Ultima, you need new Atlantic subduction. What are the best candidates for where that could start, geologically?

Candidates are usually places where there’s already compressive stress, inherited weaknesses, or nearby subduction that could “jump” or propagate. 1) Iberia–Northwest Africa (Gibraltar arc / western Mediterranean) - The region already has complex slab dynamics (subduction remnants, slab rollback) and microplate interactions. - A westward-propagating subduction zone could, in principle, begin consuming Atlantic lithosphere adjacent to Iberia or Morocco. - This is often cited because it’s one of the few places near the Atlantic with subduction-related processes today. 2) Caribbean region (Lesser Antilles / Puerto Rico trench system) - Subduction exists on the eastern margin of the Caribbean Plate (Atlantic lithosphere subducting westward). - However, it’s not a simple “Atlantic margin” trench that would neatly close the Atlantic between the Americas and Africa/Europe. It could be a seed for reorganizations that eventually produce Atlantic consumption, but it’s geometrically awkward. 3) Scotia–South Sandwich arc (southern Atlantic sector) - Active subduction consumes South Atlantic/Scotia Sea lithosphere. - Could it propagate northward or reorganize to begin consuming the South Atlantic more broadly? Possible, but again not straightforward. 4) Passive margin reactivation along eastern North America or eastern South America - In principle, if plate motions change to put those margins in compression, subduction could initiate offshore. - The challenge is that passive margins tend to be strong and buoyant; initiating a new trench requires overcoming lithospheric strength. But if old Atlantic lithosphere becomes sufficiently cold and dense, and if far-field forces become compressive, it can happen. 5) Azores–Gibraltar fracture zone system and other Atlantic transform zones - Large fracture zones are weaknesses. If stress regimes change, they can localize deformation and help start a new plate boundary. In modeling studies, subduction initiation is often parameterized rather than predicted from first principles, which is why scenarios can feel “hand-wavy.” The most defensible candidates are where subduction or strong convergence already exists near the Atlantic: the western Mediterranean and parts of the Caribbean/Scotia arcs.
Switch to Novopangaea. If the Pacific is already shrinking, why isn’t Novopangaea basically inevitable?

Because “Pacific shrinking” isn’t the same as “Pacific fully closes into one final collision geometry,” and because plate boundary reorganization can reverse trends locally. Key reasons it’s not inevitable: 1) The Pacific is not a single simple basin The “Pacific Plate” is large, but the basin includes many plates (Nazca, Cocos, Juan de Fuca, Philippine Sea, Australian, Antarctic, etc.). Some parts of the Pacific realm can shrink while others open or reorganize. 2) Spreading centers can relocate If subduction consumes oceanic lithosphere on one side, new spreading can occur elsewhere. The net area might shrink, but the geometry of closure (where the last seaway is) can vary widely. 3) Subduction can shut down Collision of buoyant features (oceanic plateaus, arcs, microcontinents) can jam subduction. If enough trenches jam, the Pacific’s net consumption could slow, and other oceans might become more important in the global plate circuit. 4) New subduction could start in the Atlantic or Indian Even if the Pacific continues shrinking, if Atlantic subduction initiates and becomes dominant, you could end up with an introversion component or even a primarily Atlantic closure. 5) True polar wander and mantle reorganization Over 250 Myr, the mantle’s convective planform can change. A persistent subduction girdle could migrate, changing which ocean is “preferred” for closure. So Novopangaea is plausible and in some sense “aligned with present boundary conditions,” but inevitability is too strong.
Give me a continent-by-continent Novopangaea sketch like you did for Pangaea Ultima.

In a Novopangaea/extroversion-style future, the Pacific closes and the Atlantic becomes the dominant ocean. 1) North America Today: - Western margin: subduction remnants in Cascadia; transform along San Andreas; subduction in Alaska–Aleutians. - Eastern margin: passive Atlantic. In Novopangaea: - North America continues to interact with Pacific trenches (Aleutians and successors). The western margin remains an active convergent boundary in many sketches. - As the Pacific narrows, North America approaches East Asia. The collision could involve Alaska–Siberia first, with later suturing farther south. - The Atlantic side stays passive and continues to widen; eastern North America remains a broad continental shelf. 2) South America Today: - Andes: major subduction zone (Nazca under South America).
