In Novopangaea: - Andean subduction persists for a long time, driving westward drift of South America relative to the mantle reference frame. - As the Pacific narrows, South America collides with Antarctica and/or Australia-derived fragments, depending on how the southern Pacific closes. - Eventual collision of South America with parts of East Antarctica or Zealandia-like crustal blocks is possible before full closure with Asia. 3) Eurasia (Europe + Asia) Today: - Eastern Asia has subduction along Japan, Kurils, Izu-Bonin-Mariana, Ryukyu, Philippines, Sunda. - Interior: complex collision belts (Himalaya, Zagros). In Novopangaea: - East Asia remains the “receiving” side of Pacific closure: arcs accrete, marginal seas close, and subduction systems migrate. - Eventually, Eurasia collides with North America across what used to be the North Pacific. - Europe remains attached to Asia; the Atlantic continues opening west of Europe, so Europe ends up facing a huge ocean. 4) Africa Today: - Converging with Eurasia in the Mediterranean; rifting in East Africa; Atlantic passive margin. In Novopangaea: - Africa continues northward and likely completes collision with Europe, eliminating the Mediterranean. - Africa’s position relative to the closing Pacific is more indirect; it may become part of the supercontinent via its attachment to Eurasia rather than directly colliding across the Pacific. - The South Atlantic remains open and widens; Africa’s west coast faces an ever-larger Atlantic. 5) Australia Today: - Moving north; interacting with Sunda subduction, New Guinea collision. In Novopangaea: - Australia becomes a key piece in Pacific closure. It collides strongly with Southeast Asia, welding onto Eurasia. - After suturing, it may act as a buttress that helps close remaining Pacific basins to the east (depending on where spreading persists). 6) Antarctica Today: - Isolated; surrounded by ocean ridges. In Novopangaea: - Antarctica may drift northward in some reconstructions, or it may remain near the pole but become involved as the southern Pacific closes. - A common sketch has Antarctica colliding with Australia (or already being connected via future plate reorganizations) and then joining the larger supercontinent assembly. 7) India and Arabia - These are already “in” Eurasia’s collision zone. In 250 Myr, they’re fully incorporated into Eurasia’s interior, contributing to thickened crust and high plateaus. Overall map feel: - A supercontinent forms where the Pacific was, roughly spanning from what used to be East Asia to the western Americas, with Australia and Antarctica attached on the south side and possibly involving complex sutures in the north. - The Atlantic is the global mega-ocean encircling the supercontinent—functionally replacing Panthalassa.
How does Amasia differ mechanically? Why would continents migrate north and close the Arctic specifically?

Amasia emphasizes convergence in the high north, which can be motivated by a few mechanisms: 1) Current northward components - North America has a motion that includes a westward component, but relative motions in the Arctic are complex. - Eurasia’s motion relative to North America in the North Atlantic/Arctic realm can evolve as the ridge–transform network changes. 2) The Arctic is small and ridge-dominated - The Arctic Ocean includes the Gakkel Ridge (slow spreading). Small ocean basins can close more readily if plate motions shift, because less oceanic lithosphere must be consumed. 3) Subduction “capture” around the margins - If subduction zones develop or migrate in the North Pacific and North Atlantic gateways, they can “pull” plates northward and rotate them such that the Arctic becomes a convergence zone. 4) Orthoversion-style mantle constraints Some researchers argue that the next supercontinent tends to form 90 degrees away from the previous due to stable mantle structures (LLSVPs) and the long-lived subduction girdle. In that view, convergence could preferentially occur in a zone that, in today’s geography, maps into the Arctic region. But Amasia is also sensitive to uncertain future behavior of the North Atlantic and subduction initiation. It’s plausible because it leverages closure of a small basin and existing complex boundaries, rather than requiring a full Atlantic-wide subduction initiation early.
If you had to rank the scenarios by plausibility given present-day tectonics, how would you do it? And justify the ranking.

With the caveat that “plausibility” depends on assumptions about subduction initiation and long-term persistence of current plate boundaries, a defensible ranking from “most aligned with today” to “requires more novel changes” might be: 1) Novopangaea-like (Pacific closure dominant) Justification: - The Pacific realm is already dominated by subduction and is shrinking in many measures. - Long-lived slab pull systems exist all around it, making continued consumption plausible. - Doesn’t require immediate, large-scale subduction initiation on passive Atlantic margins. 2) Amasia-like (Arctic closure + northern assembly) Justification: - The Arctic is a relatively small basin; modest changes in plate motions could close it. - High-latitude convergence could occur as a byproduct of continued Pacific subduction and North Atlantic/Arctic ridge adjustments. - Still does not strictly require Atlantic passive margins to become major subduction zones early. 3) Pangaea Ultima-like (Atlantic closure dominant) Justification: - Requires Atlantic subduction initiation or a major reorganization that turns passive margins into active trenches. - Not impossible, but harder to justify purely from present geometry without invoking a specific trigger (e.g., westward propagation of Mediterranean subduction into the Atlantic, or a global plate-circuit reorganization). However, the ranking can flip if you assume that supercontinent cycles tend to close interior oceans (introversion) more often, or if you believe subduction initiation along the Atlantic is likely within the next ~50–100 Myr. The biggest uncertainty is whether the Atlantic remains largely passive.
What about the role of the East African Rift? Could that change the supercontinent outcome?

Yes, it can matter, mostly by changing Africa’s rigidity and creating/consuming ocean basins that alter plate circuits. If the East African Rift succeeds: - Africa splits into the Nubian and Somali plates. - A new ocean opens in East Africa (a future “East African Ocean”), connecting to the Red Sea/Gulf of Aden system. - This introduces a new spreading ridge and transforms, changing how Africa transmits stresses between the Atlantic and Indian oceans. How that affects supercontinent futures: - In a Novopangaea world, Africa’s internal rifting might be a side show; Africa still collides with Eurasia and faces the growing Atlantic. - In a Pangaea Ultima world, rifting could either (a) delay Africa–Eurasia collision locally by changing kinematics, or (b) create a weak zone that later becomes a suture when convergence reverses, potentially producing complex internal seas and mountain belts in the final supercontinent. - In an Amasia world, the rift is again secondary, but it changes how Africa rotates as it collides with Europe and potentially as the Indian Ocean evolves. The rift’s existence also hints that Africa’s plate boundary network is actively changing today, which makes long-term predictions more uncertain.
When people draw these future maps, they often show huge mountain ranges. Where would the biggest orogens likely be in each scenario?

The biggest mountain belts tend to form where large continents collide after long-lived ocean closure—especially where thick, buoyant continental crust resists subduction. Pangaea Ultima (Atlantic closure) - Americas–Africa/Europe collision zones: - Eastern North America vs western Europe (a new Appalachians–Caledonides-style belt, but potentially more extensive). - Eastern South America vs West Africa (a major equatorial or southern-hemisphere belt). - Africa–Europe collision intensifies (Alps–Zagros–Himalaya systems may merge into a vast transcontinental orogenic plateau). - Australia–Southeast Asia collision creates a strong orogen across Indonesia/New Guinea. Novopangaea (Pacific closure) - Western Americas vs East Asia collision: - A huge belt from Alaska/Siberia through the former North Pacific closure zone. - Potentially a long cordilleran-to-Asian megabelt if collision propagates southward. - Continued collision in Asia: Himalaya/Tibet region evolves into an older, broader plateau. - Australia docking into Southeast Asia creates major mountains; later, Antarctica docking could add another suture-related belt. Amasia (Arctic closure) - North America–Eurasia collision around the Arctic produces a circum-Arctic orogen. - Existing Pacific-margin orogens may continue for a long time, but the “capstone” collision is in the north. - Additional belts form as Africa completes collision with Europe and as Australia collides with Asia. In all scenarios, older mountain belts will erode and new ones will form; so the “biggest” at 250 Myr depends on timing—whether collision is recent (high relief) or ancient (eroded to plateaus and cratonic uplands).
Let’s get more specific: in a Novopangaea scenario, what happens to the San Andreas Fault system and California? Does it keep sliding north forever?

Over 250 Myr, the current San Andreas configuration almost certainly won’t persist as-is. But we can outline plausible evolutions based on transform–subduction interactions. Today: - The San Andreas is a transform boundary between the Pacific Plate and North American Plate. - To the north, it links to the Cascadia subduction zone (Juan de Fuca subducting). - To the south, it links into the Gulf of California rift/spreading system. In a Pacific-closing, Novopangaea-like future: 1) Transform migration and consumption of microplates - The small plates (Juan de Fuca, Rivera, Cocos) are likely to be subducted or reorganized. Cascadia could shut down if the spreading centers feeding it disappear. - As those plates vanish, the transform boundary could lengthen or reconfigure. 2) Possible return to subduction along the western margin - Transform margins can evolve into subduction margins if convergence becomes more orthogonal and if there’s a mechanism to start a trench. If Pacific closure accelerates, western North America could develop new subduction segments. - Alternatively, transform motion could persist for a long time, but it won’t be stable indefinitely if the overall Pacific basin is closing. 3) California’s “translation” - In the near term (tens of Myr), coastal California continues moving northwest relative to the North American interior. - Over longer times, California could be accreted farther north (toward Alaska) or rotated as part of a complex collage of terranes. - Eventually, as North America collides with Asia, the entire margin becomes a collision zone; transform motion would be replaced by crustal shortening and thickening. So “sliding north forever” is unlikely. More likely: continued northwest translation for a geologically moderate interval, followed by boundary reconfiguration as the Pacific realm’s remaining spreading centers and trenches rearrange.
What about Baja California and the Gulf of California? Do they keep opening into a new ocean?

The Gulf of California is an active rift/spreading system tied to the Pacific–North America plate boundary. Its long-term fate depends on whether that boundary remains transform/rift-dominated or becomes convergent. Near-term extrapolation (tens of Myr): - Baja California continues moving with the Pacific Plate. - The Gulf could continue to open and lengthen, potentially extending rifting northward into California (as has happened episodically in the past). Long-term (hundreds of Myr): - If the Pacific basin is closing and western North America becomes more convergent, the Gulf spreading could slow, stop, or be inverted (compressed). - If transform motion remains dominant for a long time, the Gulf could evolve into a more mature oceanic basin—but it’s hard to keep a small ocean open while the larger surrounding basin is being consumed unless plate motions support it. In a Novopangaea endmember, eventually the western margin becomes part of a convergent/collisional system, so the Gulf’s spreading is unlikely to persist all the way to 250 Myr uninterrupted.
People also talk about Zealandia. In these futures, does Zealandia matter, or is it just a minor block?

Zealandia can matter disproportionately because microcontinents and continental fragments often become key “stitches” in collision zones and can alter subduction geometry. Today: - Zealandia (including New Zealand and submerged continental crust) sits near the boundary between the Pacific and Australian plates. - It’s involved in subduction (Hikurangi) and transform motion (Alpine Fault) and is near the Tonga–Kermadec subduction system. In future scenarios: - Novopangaea: Zealandia is likely to be accreted to Australia and/or to a larger Asia–Australia collage as the southwest Pacific reorganizes. It could become part of a major suture zone if it collides during Pacific closure. - Pangaea Ultima: Zealandia’s role is more peripheral, but it could still be caught in Australia’s northward collision and help shape the Indonesian orogen. - Amasia: similar to Novopangaea in the sense that Pacific-margin processes remain important; Zealandia could be consumed or sutured depending on whether it rides with the Australian Plate or is fragmented. So it’s “minor” in area relative to major continents, but it can have an outsized influence on where trenches lock, where arcs collide, and how the final supercontinent margin looks.
Can we talk about the timescale? What plate speeds are reasonable to assume over 250 million years? Do plates keep moving at a few cm/year?

Plate speeds of a few cm/yr are typical today, and over geologic time they vary but are often in the range of ~1–10 cm/yr for major plates, with episodes faster or slower. Over 250 Myr: - At 2 cm/yr, a plate moves ~5,000 km. - At 5 cm/yr, ~12,500 km. - At 8 cm/yr, ~20,000 km. That’s enough to rearrange oceans completely. But you shouldn’t assume constant velocity or direction: - Plate motions change with boundary reorganizations (ridge jumps, subduction initiation/cessation, continental collision). - As continents collide, convergence can slow because buoyant crust resists subduction, and deformation becomes distributed. So “a few cm/yr” is a good order-of-magnitude, but trajectories and rates will not be steady.
Does true polar wander complicate these maps? Like, could the whole solid Earth reorient so the supercontinent ends up at different latitudes?

Yes. True polar wander (TPW) is the reorientation of Earth’s solid outer shell relative to the spin axis, driven by changes in the planet’s mass distribution (e.g., large-scale mantle convection, subduction, plume activity). Implications for future maps: - Even if plate motions relative to one another are predicted, absolute latitude/longitude can shift due to TPW. - The supercontinent could end up more equatorial or more polar than a simple plate-motion extrapolation suggests. - Climate implications are significant because latitude controls insolation; but the tectonic assembly geometry (who collides with whom) is less sensitive than the final latitude. In practice, many future supercontinent illustrations either ignore TPW or implicitly choose a mantle reference frame that minimizes it. For 250 Myr, TPW could be nontrivial.
I want to understand the “orthoversion” idea more. How does it predict where the next supercontinent forms?

Orthoversion is a hypothesis about supercontinent formation relative to the previous supercontinent’s position and the mantle’s long-lived structures. Core concept: - After a supercontinent forms, subduction tends to encircle it (a “subduction girdle”). - Over time, slabs sink into the mantle and may accumulate in certain regions, influencing mantle convection patterns. - The next supercontinent tends to form not by simply reversing breakup (introversion) or closing the exterior ocean (extroversion), but by assembling over a region roughly 90 degrees away from the previous supercontinent’s center in a mantle reference frame. Why 90 degrees? - The idea is that the subduction girdle and associated downwellings establish a convection planform that favors upwellings under the former supercontinent and downwellings around it. The next locus of assembly is offset. How it maps to today: - If you take Pangaea’s approximate center and rotate 90 degrees, you land in a region that could correspond to the Arctic/northern Pacific realm in some reconstructions, which aligns with Amasia-like outcomes. - But the “90 degrees” is not a strict geometric law; it’s a statistical tendency proposed from limited supercontinent cycles. So orthoversion provides a mantle-dynamics argument for Amasia or hybrid outcomes, rather than a purely plate-boundary extrapolation.
If someone asked “what will Earth look like in 250 Myr,” is it better to talk about multiple scenarios or pick one? For a map-making purpose, what’s most defensible?

For a single illustrative map, the most defensible approach is to present one scenario clearly labeled as “one plausible outcome,” plus a brief explanation of alternatives and uncertainties. If you can present multiple panels, even better. If forced to pick one: - A Pacific-closure dominant map (Novopangaea-like) is often easiest to justify from present-day boundary conditions because the Pacific has extensive subduction and is already being consumed. - But a responsible map should still note that Atlantic subduction initiation or Arctic closure could redirect the system. For map-making defensibility: - Anchor the map in known plate velocities and directions for the next ~20–50 Myr (where extrapolations are more credible). - Beyond that, switch to boundary-logic: where subduction is likely to persist, where collisions are inevitable (Africa–Europe, Australia–SE Asia), and where new boundaries might form. - Avoid precise coastlines and exact lat/long; show broad continental outlines and major mountain belts and ocean basins.
Let’s do a more “quantitative” thought experiment. If the Pacific is shrinking at some net rate, can we estimate when it closes?

You can do a back-of-the-envelope, but it’s very rough because “Pacific shrinking” depends on which cross-section you measure and how you define net area change. A crude approach: - Take an average width of the Pacific basin along a transect, say ~10,000–15,000 km in some places today. - Suppose the effective convergence rate between the Americas and Asia across the Pacific is on the order of a few cm/yr, say 3–6 cm/yr (varies by latitude and plate pair). Time to close along that transect: - 10,000 km / (5 cm/yr) = 10,000,000 m / (5e-2 m/yr) = 200 million years. - If 3 cm/yr, ~333 million years. - If 6 cm/yr, ~167 million years. But: - Closure won’t be uniform; some parts close earlier (north or south) depending on plate motions and intervening microplates. - Spreading within the Pacific realm can offset closure locally. - Collisions can slow convergence. So “order 200–300 Myr” is a plausible timescale for major Pacific closure, which is why 250 Myr is often used as a benchmark for next-supercontinent illustrations.
How do hotspots factor into this? Like Hawaii—does the Hawaiian-Emperor chain tell us anything about future motions?

Hotspot tracks primarily inform past plate motions relative to a mantle reference frame. They can help constrain absolute plate motion and changes in direction (like the Hawaiian-Emperor bend). For the future: - If hotspots are relatively fixed in the deep mantle (a debated approximation), then the direction of hotspot tracks can suggest the absolute motion of plates today. - But predicting future plate motions from hotspot tracks is limited because plate boundary forces can change. Hotspots don’t “drive” plates strongly; they’re more like markers. Hawaii specifically: - It records Pacific Plate motion over tens of millions of years. - The Pacific Plate’s future depends on the evolution of surrounding subduction zones. If those trenches continue to consume the Pacific Plate, the hotspot track continues until the plate segment carrying the hotspot path is subducted or deformed near a boundary. So hotspots help you frame the kinematics, but they don’t uniquely determine whether the Pacific or Atlantic closes.
In these future worlds, what happens to the Mediterranean region? Is it guaranteed to vanish?

It’s not absolutely guaranteed, but it’s one of the more robust near-future tectonic expectations because Africa–Eurasia convergence is ongoing. Reasons it likely vanishes (on geologic timescales): - Africa is moving generally northward relative to Eurasia. - Subduction zones in the Mediterranean consume oceanic and transitional crust. - Collision has already occurred in many segments (Alps, Dinarides, parts of Anatolia), and the remaining basins are relatively small. Possible exceptions/complications: - Microplate dynamics can maintain small basins temporarily (back-arc extension in the Aegean, for instance). - Slab rollback can open basins even while overall convergence continues, but those basins tend to be transient. Over 250 Myr, the odds strongly favor the Mediterranean being replaced by a collision zone and mountain belts, regardless of which supercontinent scenario you pick.
What about the Red Sea and Gulf of Aden? Do they just keep opening into a big ocean?

In the short term, they likely continue opening because they’re active rifts/spreading centers. Over longer times: - If the East African Rift evolves into full seafloor spreading, the Red Sea–Gulf of Aden system could connect to a larger ocean basin in East Africa. - But whether that becomes a “big ocean” depends on whether it avoids being closed later by changes in plate motions. New oceans can be short-lived if later convergence inverts them. In many supercontinent futures: - Africa’s collision with Eurasia changes stress fields and could modify rift dynamics. - If the Indian Ocean’s plate circuit reorganizes, spreading could slow or shift. So it could become a sizable ocean for a time, but persistence for 250 Myr is uncertain.
I’m trying to visualize coastlines. Are future coastlines even meaningful to draw, given sea level changes and erosion?

Detailed coastlines are the least meaningful part of deep-time maps. Why: - Sea level can change by hundreds of meters due to changes in mid-ocean ridge volume, glaciation (if any), sedimentation, and basin subsidence/uplift. - Erosion and sedimentation can radically reshape continental margins. - Tectonic uplift/subsidence, especially near collision zones, creates new basins and destroys old ones. What is meaningful: - Broad continental outlines (cratons and major terranes). - Location of major rifts, sutures, mountain belts. - Distribution of major ocean basins versus interior seas. So if you draw coastlines, they should be stylized and secondary to tectonic features.
Could you describe what the climate might be like on Pangaea Ultima vs Novopangaea? I know it’s speculative, but tie it to geography.

Climate is speculative because atmospheric composition, solar luminosity, ocean circulation, and biosphere feedbacks all matter. But geography alone implies some robust tendencies. Common supercontinent climate features: - Strong continentality: interiors are far from moisture sources, leading to arid deserts and large seasonal temperature ranges. - Monsoonal circulation: large landmasses heat/cool strongly, driving seasonal winds. - Reduced coastline-to-area ratio: fewer shallow seas, potentially affecting marine productivity and heat transport. Pangaea Ultima (equatorial-ish supercontinent) - If the supercontinent straddles the equator, you might get intense monsoons on the margins and very arid interiors. - If a large ocean remains on one side, strong coastal precipitation gradients occur. - Mountain belts from multiple collisions could create rain shadows and high plateaus influencing circulation. Novopangaea (supercontinent in former Pacific region, huge Atlantic) - A giant Atlantic could provide an enormous moisture reservoir and strong ocean currents, but depending on gateways, circulation could be very different. - If the supercontinent is more equator-to-midlatitude spanning, you still get monsoons, but the distribution depends on where major mountain ranges sit (e.g., an immense cordilleran-Asian belt). - Polar configuration matters: if Antarctica docks and polar seaways are closed, that changes heat transport. In both cases, increased solar luminosity in 250 Myr (~a few percent brighter) could make Earth warmer if greenhouse gases are not lower; but CO₂ could be regulated by silicate weathering, which itself depends on mountain building. So geography and tectonics feed back into climate.
Some popular sources claim Pangaea Ultima would cause mass extinction due to extreme heat. Is that a solid conclusion?

It’s not a solid conclusion; it’s a plausible risk factor in some model setups, but it depends on assumptions. Why a supercontinent could be stressful: - Large interior deserts and heat extremes. - Potential reduction in coastal habitats and shallow seas. - Changes in ocean circulation that could affect oxygenation. - If volcanism is elevated (e.g., large igneous provinces), greenhouse gases could rise. Why “inevitable mass extinction” is too strong: - Climate depends heavily on atmospheric composition, cloud feedbacks, ocean gateways, and biological adaptation. - Supercontinents have existed without always triggering the most severe extinctions; extinctions often correlate with specific triggers (massive volcanism, rapid climate change, ocean anoxia) rather than supercontinent geometry alone. - Some modeling studies that get extreme heat assume high CO₂ or particular circulation constraints. So the geography could contribute to harsh conditions, but it’s not deterministically catastrophic.
Let’s go back to mechanics. How do continental collisions actually stop an ocean from subducting? Why does buoyant crust jam trenches?

Subduction works best when dense oceanic lithosphere sinks into the mantle. Continental lithosphere is thicker and more buoyant, especially the crustal portion. When a continent arrives at a trench: 1) Density contrast - Oceanic lithosphere: basaltic crust + cold mantle lithosphere; becomes dense as it cools. - Continental crust: felsic/intermediate composition; lower density, resists sinking. 2) Mechanical strength and thickness - Continents have thick crust (30–70+ km in orogens) that is hard to bend into a subduction zone. - The incoming buoyant block increases coupling and resists slab pull. 3) Slab breakoff and polarity changes - The oceanic slab attached to the continent may detach (“slab breakoff”), removing slab pull and altering forces. - Subduction can “jump” to a new location (subduction polarity reversal), or transform boundaries can form. 4) Accretion and crustal thickening - Instead of continuing to subduct, material is scraped off and stacked, forming mountain belts and plateaus. - The suture zone becomes a locus of deformation rather than a site of continued oceanic consumption (because there’s no ocean left there). This is why the last stages of ocean closure are complex and why plate motions can reorganize around collisions.
In a Pacific-closure future, which collision happens first: Australia with SE Asia, or North America with Asia?

Australia–SE Asia collision is likely to occur earlier than a full North America–Asia collision, because it’s already underway in a sense. - Australia is moving north at relatively fast rates (historically among the faster continental motions). - The Indonesian region is a complex convergent margin with active subduction and arc-continent collision (New Guinea, Banda arc complexities). - Many reconstructions suggest progressive suturing over the next tens of millions of years. North America–Asia collision across the North Pacific requires: - Continued consumption of large swaths of Pacific basin. - Complex interactions with the Aleutian/Kuril/Japan trench systems and possible reorganization. This is more likely on a >100 Myr timescale. So if you’re sequencing events: Australia’s docking into Asia is a “nearer-term” tectonic inevitability than a full trans-Pacific continental collision.
What happens to Japan in these long-term futures? Does it get smashed into Asia or drift away?

Japan sits on a convergent margin with subduction and back-arc processes, so its fate is tied to trench migration and arc accretion. Today: - Subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath Japan (Japan Trench) and of the Philippine Sea Plate in the south. - Back-arc basins like the Sea of Japan formed by extension behind the arc. Long term: - Island arcs often accrete to continents. Japan could be welded to the Asian margin as subduction continues and back-arc basins close. - Alternatively, ongoing subduction and rollback could keep an arc separated by a marginal sea for a long time, but over 250 Myr, it’s more likely that arc terranes are incorporated into the continent in some configuration. In a Pacific-closure scenario: - Japan is likely to be part of the collisional “stitching” between Asia and whatever approaches from the east (North America or terranes), either being accreted and uplifted or being heavily deformed. So “smashed into Asia” (accreted) is a reasonable expectation over that timescale.
You mentioned “ridge jumps.” Can you explain what that means and why it matters for future oceans?

A ridge jump is when the locus of seafloor spreading shifts from one position to another, effectively relocating where new oceanic crust is created. Mechanisms: - Changes in plate boundary forces can cause a spreading center to propagate, abandon an old segment, and establish a new one. - Hotspots/plumes can weaken lithosphere and encourage new rifts. - Interaction with subduction zones can reorganize plate boundaries. Why it matters: - It changes which ocean basin grows and which shrinks. - It can transfer blocks of crust from one plate to another. - It can create new microplates and alter the geometry of future collisions. Example conceptually: - If a ridge jump occurs in the Atlantic, it could change the relative motions of the Americas and Africa/Europe, affecting whether the Atlantic continues opening. - In the Pacific realm, ridge jumps can rearrange microplates and change closure pathways. Over 250 Myr, multiple ridge jumps are likely, making precise predictions difficult.
How do geologists test these future-supercontinent hypotheses? It’s not like we can observe it.

They test them indirectly through consistency with physical principles, present-day observations, and analogs from the past. 1) Plate kinematic modeling - Use present plate motions and constraints to project forward for a limited time (tens of Myr). - Beyond that, explore scenario-based modeling with plausible boundary conditions. 2) Geodynamic simulations - Numerical models of mantle convection coupled to lithospheric plates examine how subduction, slab pull, and mantle flow could evolve. - These can produce tendencies (e.g., where subduction initiates, whether interior oceans close). 3) Paleogeographic/paleomagnetic analogs - Study how previous supercontinents assembled and broke apart: Rodinia, Pannotia, Pangaea. - Look for statistical patterns in where assembly occurs relative to mantle structures. 4) Seismic tomography and mantle structure - Present mantle images show slabs and LLSVPs, which may persist and influence future convection patterns. 5) Sensitivity analyses - Vary uncertain parameters (lithospheric strength, viscosity, yield criteria) to see which outcomes are robust. They’re not “predictions” in the strict sense; they’re physically informed scenarios.
Can you outline a plausible hybrid scenario that combines elements of Amasia and Novopangaea?

One plausible hybrid is: Pacific continues shrinking (Novopangaea tendency), but the first major continental suturing that creates a supercontinent nucleus happens in the high north (Amasia tendency). Sketch: 1) Australia docks into SE Asia relatively early, welding to Eurasia. 2) The Arctic Ocean closes as North America rotates/advances toward Eurasia; Greenland and Arctic basins are consumed or transformed into mountain belts. 3) The North Pacific narrows substantially; Alaska/Siberia collision becomes a major suture, creating a “northern cap” of connected land. 4) Southward, the Pacific still exists as a diminishing basin for a long time, with continuing subduction along the Americas. 5) Eventually, the remaining Pacific closes farther south, completing a supercontinent that is initially Arctic-centered but later extends into midlatitudes. In this hybrid, the Atlantic can still widen for much of the interval, and the final supercontinent might not be centered exactly where classic Novopangaea maps place it.
In that hybrid, where does Africa end up? Still colliding with Europe?

Yes—Africa colliding with Europe is compatible with almost any scenario because it’s already underway. In the hybrid: - Africa completes collision with Europe and becomes part of the Eurasian “core” well before final Pacific closure. - Africa’s west margin continues facing the Atlantic, which may widen. - Depending on how the Indian Ocean evolves and whether East Africa rifts fully, Africa’s internal geometry could change, but it remains attached to Eurasia in the assembled landmass. So Africa becomes an integral part of the growing Eurasia-centered supercontinent nucleus, even if the final closure involves the Pacific and/or Arctic.
What about South America’s fate in an Amasia-like world? Does it also go north?

South America could drift northward relative to Antarctica and Africa depending on plate reorganizations, but its dominant near-term driver is the Andean subduction system on the west. In an Amasia-like assembly: - Northward motion of the Americas (especially North America) is emphasized because the key closure is in the Arctic. - South America might: - Remain connected to North America (as today) and be carried northward as part of the same plate system, though internal deformation could occur. - Or undergo changes if the Caribbean and surrounding plate boundaries reorganize. Over 250 Myr: - It’s plausible that South America ends up at higher latitudes than today, but whether it becomes a central piece of the Arctic-assembled supercontinent or remains more peripheral depends on how the Pacific and Atlantic evolve. - Many Amasia sketches focus on the northern hemisphere assembly and leave the southern continents (South America, Africa, Antarctica) more dispersed or attached later.
How do transforms and fracture zones show up in future maps? Do they matter at supercontinent scales?

At the largest scale, transforms are secondary compared to ridges and trenches, but they matter in three ways: 1) Weakness inheritance Fracture zones and transforms are long-lived mechanical and thermal anomalies. They can localize later deformation and sometimes guide where new plate boundaries form. 2) Microplate formation and terrane transport Transforms can slice off blocks, create microplates, and transport crust laterally—important for the detailed stitching of supercontinents. 3) Ocean basin segmentation Transforms segment mid-ocean ridges, affecting ridge geometry and the distribution of seafloor ages, which can influence where subduction might initiate (older seafloor is denser and more likely to subduct). So on a supercontinent-scale cartoon map, you might not draw them, but in a more technical reconstruction they help determine where boundaries could reorganize.
If the Atlantic keeps opening, could it become a “new Pacific” in terms of having lots of subduction around it?

Potentially, yes—over long timescales an ocean basin can transition from a mostly passive-margin ocean to one with extensive subduction, but the transition is not guaranteed. For the Atlantic to become “Pacific-like”: - Subduction would need to initiate along its margins and become widespread. - Over time, as the Atlantic seafloor ages and cools, it becomes denser and more subductable. - Changes in plate motions could put passive margins into compression, favoring trench formation. If the Pacific closes in a Novopangaea scenario: - The Atlantic becomes the dominant global ocean, and eventually it might acquire more subduction as part of the next cycle (after the next supercontinent forms and later breaks apart). - So yes, the Atlantic could become the next major subduction-ringed ocean, but that’s likely beyond the 250 Myr horizon and more in the realm of the subsequent supercontinent cycle.
What are the biggest unknowns that could “flip” us from one scenario to another?

The major flip factors are: 1) Subduction initiation on passive margins If Atlantic-margin subduction starts and becomes self-sustaining, introversion/Pangaea Ultima becomes far more plausible. 2) Persistence vs shutdown of key subduction zones If major Pacific subduction systems shut down due to collision/jamming, Pacific closure could slow, allowing other oceans to dominate. 3) Plate circuit reorganizations Changes in spreading centers and plate boundaries can alter relative motions drastically. For example, if a ridge is captured or a new plate forms, directions can change. 4) Mantle convection reorganization If downwellings and upwellings shift, they can influence rifting and convergence patterns. 5) Continental breakup events New rifts (e.g., East Africa) that become full oceans can change geometry and stress distribution. 6) Accretion of oceanic plateaus and arcs Large buoyant features can jam trenches and force subduction jumps—changing closure pathways. Those uncertainties are why scenario-based thinking is appropriate.
If you were drawing a future map for a textbook, what features would you label to make it educational rather than just art?

I’d label tectonic elements and kinematic logic rather than just continent names. For any chosen scenario: - Major plate boundaries: subduction zones (with teeth), spreading ridges, transforms. - Sutures: collision zones and major orogenic belts. - Major ocean basins: which ones are expanding vs shrinking. - Key gateways: seaways that control ocean circulation (e.g., whether a circum-polar seaway exists). - Approximate motion vectors for major plates. - Regions of likely volcanism: subduction arcs, rift zones, hotspots (optional). - Uncertainty notes: “possible subduction initiation here” zones. That makes the map a tectonic argument rather than a single asserted outcome.
Let’s do one more deep dive: in Pangaea Ultima, if the Atlantic closes, what happens to the Mid-Atlantic Ridge? Does it just get subducted?

If the Atlantic transitions from opening to closing, the Mid-Atlantic Ridge’s fate depends on where subduction initiates and how the plate circuit reorganizes. Typical sequence in an introversion-style closure: 1) Subduction initiates at one or both Atlantic margins. - Once a trench forms and starts consuming Atlantic lithosphere, the relative motion between the adjacent continents changes from divergence to convergence. 2) Spreading slows and may cease. - If convergence across the Atlantic becomes dominant, the ridge may become extinct (no longer producing new crust). 3) Ridge consumption or capture - The extinct ridge becomes part of one plate or the other and can be carried toward a trench. - Eventually it can be subducted, but ridges are hot and buoyant, which can complicate subduction (they can cause slab windows, changes in volcanism, and reorganization of trench geometry). 4) Final collision - After most oceanic lithosphere is consumed, continents collide and the remaining ridge fragments are incorporated into the suture zone or subducted earlier. So yes, in principle it can be subducted, but the process is messy and can involve ridge jumps and microplate formation.
Would ridge subduction create unusual volcanism? Like slab windows?

Yes. When a spreading ridge is subducted, the two diverging plates that used to be separated by the ridge can create a “slab window”—a gap in the subducted slab where hot asthenosphere can rise. Consequences: - Anomalous volcanism inland from the trench, sometimes basaltic and hotspot-like in geochemistry. - Changes in arc volcanism: reduced water flux from slab, altered magma composition. - Thermal effects: increased heat flow, metamorphism. We see analogs in Earth history (e.g., ridge-trench interactions along the western Americas). So in an Atlantic-closure future, if the Mid-Atlantic Ridge is subducted, you’d expect unusual magmatic episodes near the consuming margin.
I think I’ve got the main scenarios. Now, could you summarize the “most likely” arrangement of the continents in 250 Myr in a single coherent narrative—without naming the scenarios?

Over the next 250 million years, the most physically consistent narrative with today’s plate boundaries is that the planet continues to concentrate subduction around the Pacific realm while the Atlantic remains a major spreading ocean for a long interval. Australia completes its northward collision with Southeast Asia, thickening crust and welding into the Eurasian margin. Africa finishes closing the remaining Mediterranean basins and becomes fully sutured to Europe and western Asia, expanding the already vast Eurasian collage of collision belts. Meanwhile, continued consumption of oceanic lithosphere around the Pacific progressively narrows the ocean between the western Americas and East Asia; island arcs and microcontinents are scraped off and added to continental margins, and long mountain chains develop along the eventual collision zones. In the far north, convergence between North America and Eurasia intensifies as the remaining seaways shrink, producing a broad Arctic-to-North-Pacific suture. By the end of the interval, most major continental blocks are joined into a single, immense landmass assembled where the Pacific used to be, encircled by a dominant global ocean occupying much of the former Atlantic and Indian realms, with sprawling orogenic belts marking the final sutures and broad interior regions far from the moderating influence of the sea.
That was good. One last thing: if I wanted to read more, what keywords should I search that are technical enough to avoid pop-sci?

Useful technical search terms: - “supercontinent cycle introversion extroversion orthoversion” - “Amasia supercontinent model” - “Novopangaea future plate tectonic reconstruction” - “Pangaea Ultima subduction initiation Atlantic” - “subduction initiation passive margin mechanics” - “mantle convection LLSVP supercontinent formation” - “plate circuit reorganization ridge jump” - “slab rollback Mediterranean Gibraltar arc” - “global plate reconstruction 250 Ma future” - “geodynamic modeling of supercontinent assembly” Searching those alongside “geodynamics,” “numerical modeling,” or “tectonic reconstruction” will generally surface more technical literature and conference material.
Is the Mercator projection really that misleading? Show me how wrong our mental model of country sizes is.

Yes—Mercator is “misleading” if you use it for comparing areas, because it’s conformal (preserves local angles/shapes) at the expense of area. The distortion explodes as you move away from the equator, because the scale factor is sec(latitude). That means at 60° latitude, linear scale is doubled; area scale is quadrupled. At 80°, linear scale is about 5.76×; area is about 33×. So Greenland, Canada, Russia, Scandinavia—everything high-latitude—gets visually inflated. A quick mental reset with a few hard comparisons (true areas, roughly): - Africa: ~30.37 million km² - Greenland: ~2.16 million km² Africa is about 14 times Greenland, yet on Mercator Greenland can look comparable or even larger depending on the map’s cropping and centering. - South America: ~17.84 million km² - Europe: ~10.18 million km² South America is ~1.75× Europe, but on Mercator Europe often “feels” similar in size. - India: ~3.29 million km² - Alaska: ~1.72 million km² India is almost 2× Alaska, but Mercator can make Alaska look gigantic. If you want to “see” how wrong the mental model is without images, we can do it in a structured way: pick a few latitudes and compute the Mercator area inflation factor, then apply it to countries that sit mostly at those latitudes. Or we can compare Mercator to equal-area projections like Gall–Peters, Mollweide, Eckert IV, or Equal Earth and talk about what each sacrifices. Want to go country-by-country (e.g., Greenland vs Africa, Russia vs Africa, Europe vs South America), or do you want the projection math first so the “why” is mind-blowing instead of just the “what”?
Give me the projection math first, but keep it intuitive. I want to feel the distortion, not just memorize formulas.

Mercator’s core idea: make compass navigation easy. If you draw a straight line on the map, it corresponds to a constant compass bearing (a rhumb line). To accomplish that, the projection must preserve angles locally—so it’s conformal. Here’s the intuitive math in layers: 1) Think of Earth as a sphere (good enough for intuition). Let latitude be φ (phi), longitude be λ (lambda). On the globe: - Going a tiny bit east-west at latitude φ covers a distance proportional to cos(φ). - Going a tiny bit north-south covers a distance proportional to 1 (in angular terms). So as you go toward the poles, east-west distances “shrink” on the real Earth because circles of latitude get smaller by cos(φ). 2) Mercator’s trick: stretch the map so that local shapes aren’t squished. If you map longitude linearly (x ∝ λ), then the east-west scale on the map is constant for a given Δλ. But on the Earth, that same Δλ corresponds to a shorter real distance at higher φ (because cos φ). So to keep shapes correct, Mercator must also stretch north-south distances by the same factor that east-west distances are being stretched. That factor is 1/cos(φ) = sec(φ). So: - Linear scale factor k(φ) = sec(φ) - Area scale factor A(φ) = sec²(φ) This is the “feel it” moment: at 0° (equator), sec(0)=1 → no distortion. At 60°, sec(60)=2 → everything is twice as tall and twice as wide locally → four times the area. At 80°, sec(80)≈5.76 → ~33× area. 3) Why does the map shoot to infinity near the poles? Because cos(φ) approaches 0 as φ→90°. So sec(φ)→∞. Mercator can’t even include the poles: they are at infinite y-values. 4) The actual y formula (still intuitive): Mercator uses: x = R * λ y = R * ln(tan(π/4 + φ/2)) That ln(tan(...)) part is basically “how much cumulative stretching you need” as you move north. It’s the integral of sec(φ). In other words, the y-coordinate is the sum of all the little stretches required to keep angles correct. If you want a visceral sense: imagine wrapping a cylinder around Earth at the equator (that’s like mapping longitude to x). Now to keep shapes consistent, you have to “pull” the cylinder’s vertical direction more and more as you move away from the equator. That pulling grows without bound approaching the poles. If you give me a few latitudes where you think certain countries “sit” (or we can estimate), we can compute how inflated they appear on Mercator compared to true area. Or we can talk about why your brain learns the wrong “sizes” from a world map that’s basically a “latitude inflation machine.”
Okay, now mind-blow me with some specific comparisons, but do it like a guided tour. Like “you think X is bigger than Y, but actually…”

Let’s do a guided “size hallucination tour,” anchored in true areas (approx) and then the Mercator vibe that misleads you. Stop 1: Greenland vs Africa (the classic reality check) - Greenland: ~2.16 million km² - Africa: ~30.37 million km² Reality: Africa ≈ 14× Greenland. Why Mercator breaks your intuition: Greenland sits mostly around 60–83°N. Take a representative latitude like ~72°N. sec(72°) ≈ 3.24 → area inflation ≈ 10.5×. Africa straddles the equator; a lot of it is between ~35°N and ~35°S. Representative latitude near 0–15° gives area inflation maybe ~1.0–1.07×. So on a Mercator world map, Greenland gets “puffed up” by roughly an order of magnitude relative to equatorial regions, and Africa barely changes. That’s how you end up with the psychological lie “Greenland is Africa-sized.” It’s not even close. Stop 2: Europe vs South America - Europe: ~10.18 million km² - South America: ~17.84 million km² Reality: South America ≈ 1.75× Europe. But Europe’s “center of mass” latitude is high: large parts are ~45–60°N. At 55°: sec(55°)≈1.74 → area inflation ≈ 3.0×. South America spans equator down to ~55°S, but much of it is tropical/subtropical. Representative maybe ~15–25°S: At 20°: sec(20°)≈1.06 → area inflation ≈ 1.13×. So Europe is “visually promoted” by ~2–3× compared to tropical land. That can invert your gut sense, making Europe feel comparable to South America. Stop 3: Alaska vs India - Alaska: ~1.72 million km² - India: ~3.29 million km² Reality: India ≈ 1.9× Alaska. Alaska sits around ~55–70°N. Choose ~62°: sec(62°)≈2.13 → area inflation ≈ 4.5×. India sits around ~8–35°N. Choose ~22°: sec(22°)≈1.08 → area inflation ≈ 1.17×. So on Mercator, Alaska can “feel” multiple times India even though it’s about half. Stop 4: Russia vs Africa (the “biggest country” confusion) - Russia: ~17.1 million km² - Africa: ~30.37 million km² Reality: Africa ≈ 1.78× Russia. Russia has huge high-latitude extent (50–75°N). Representative ~60°N gives area inflation 4×. Africa mostly low-latitude gives ~1×. So Russia can look “Africa-ish” or larger on Mercator even though Africa is vastly bigger. Stop 5: Antarctica (the “map monster”) - Antarctica: ~14.0 million km² Reality: smaller than Africa, smaller than Asia, but still huge. Mercator can’t even show it properly because it approaches the pole; it would blow up to infinity. Most Mercator maps just cut it off, which is its own distortion: you don’t even get a chance to calibrate your brain. If you want to keep the tour going, we can do “country pair battles” you choose (e.g., “Is Australia bigger than Europe?” “Is Mexico bigger than Greenland?” “Is Indonesia bigger than…”). Or we can do an even more mind-bending step: compare the Mercator inflation at the north edge vs the south edge of a single country, so you see how even within one nation the map lies.
Let’s do that within-one-country thing. Pick a long north-south country and show how Mercator stretches it unevenly.

A perfect example is Chile: it’s famously long north–south, spanning roughly from about 17°S down to about 56°S. On the globe, Chile is long, but its “width” (east–west) is relatively narrow. On Mercator, the southern part gets exaggerated more than the northern part, because the scale factor depends on latitude. Let’s compute representative Mercator scale factors: Mercator linear scale k(φ) = sec(φ) = 1/cos(φ). - Near Arica (north Chile) ~18°S: cos(18°) ≈ 0.9511 → sec ≈ 1.0514 So linear dimensions are ~5% inflated; area ~1.105× (≈10% inflated). - Near Santiago ~33°S: cos(33°) ≈ 0.8387 → sec ≈ 1.1925 Linear ~19% inflated; area ~1.42× (42% inflated). - Near Punta Arenas ~53°S: cos(53°) ≈ 0.6018 → sec ≈ 1.661 Linear ~66% inflated; area ~2.76×. - Near Cape Horn vicinity ~56°S: cos(56°) ≈ 0.5592 → sec ≈ 1.788 Linear ~79% inflated; area ~3.20×. What that means visually: the far south of Chile is “zoomed in” compared to the north. If you measure a 100 km “real-world” distance in northern Chile and compare it to a 100 km distance in far southern Chile, the southern one will occupy significantly more map space. It’s like the map is using a different zoom level depending on latitude—because it is. This is why on Mercator, Patagonia and Tierra del Fuego look roomier and more expansive than your mental model expects, and why southern mid-latitude regions can feel “bigger” than they truly are relative to the tropics. If you want a more extreme example, we can do Norway (58°N to 71°N) or Russia (41°N to 82°N) where the scale change becomes dramatic: north Norway is literally at a different “map zoom” than south Norway.
Do Norway next. And make it dramatic with numbers. Like show the ratio between the scale at Oslo and the scale at Tromsø.

Norway is great because it spans a big latitude range and sits high enough that Mercator’s sec(φ) factor climbs fast. Approx latitudes: - Oslo: ~59.9°N (call it 60°N for clean math) - Tromsø: ~69.7°N (call it 70°N) Mercator linear scale factor: k(φ) = sec(φ) At 60°: cos(60°) = 0.5 → sec(60°) = 2.0 So every local distance is doubled (compared to equator-scale). At 70°: cos(70°) ≈ 0.342 → sec(70°) ≈ 2.924 So every local distance is inflated by ~2.924. Now the dramatic part: compare Tromsø scale to Oslo scale: k(70)/k(60) ≈ 2.924 / 2.0 = 1.462 So Mercator is effectively ~46% more zoomed-in around Tromsø than around Oslo. Area distortion comparison: A(φ) = sec²(φ) At 60°: area factor = 4.0 At 70°: area factor ≈ (2.924)² ≈ 8.55 Ratio of area inflation Tromsø vs Oslo: 8.55 / 4.0 ≈ 2.14 So patches of land near Tromsø are shown with more than double the area (relative to each other) compared to patches near Oslo—on the same map. This is the weird psychological effect: you look at a single country and subconsciously assume the “scale” is uniform. In Mercator it isn’t. Northern Norway is literally given more canvas per square kilometer than southern Norway. If you want to push it further: compare Oslo (~60°) to Svalbard (~78°N). At 78°: cos≈0.208 → sec≈4.81 → area≈23.1 Compared to Oslo’s 4.0, that’s almost 5.8× more area inflation. So on a Mercator map that includes Svalbard, it looks like someone turned on “giant mode.”
That’s wild. Now switch gears: why do people keep using Mercator if it’s that distortive? Give me the practical reasons, but also the cultural inertia.

Practical reasons: 1) Conformal = shapes and angles look “right” locally For many map-reading tasks—especially at city/region scales—preserving angles makes roads, coastlines, and bearings visually consistent. People often interpret “looks right” as “is right.” 2) Straight rhumb lines (constant compass bearing) On Mercator, a loxodrome is a straight line. Historically, that mattered a lot for navigation with a compass: you could plot a constant bearing as a straight segment and follow it. (Great-circle routes aren’t straight on Mercator, but rhumb lines were operationally convenient.) 3) It tiles nicely in web mapping This is the modern, huge reason: the “Web Mercator” projection (EPSG:3857) became the de facto standard for slippy maps. Reasons: - It’s mathematically convenient: x and y relate cleanly to longitude and latitude (with a spherical approximation). - It supports square tiles, easy zoom levels, and consistent tiling schemes. - It keeps local shapes reasonable, which helps with street maps and user expectations. Cultural inertia: 1) Familiarity becomes “truth” If you see the same world map in classrooms, atlases, and media for years, your brain internalizes it as the default geometry of the world. Alternatives look “wrong” because they violate the learned picture, even if they’re more accurate for area. 2) Conflation of aesthetics with accuracy Mercator makes mid-latitude countries look big and powerful; it also makes coastlines and continents look pleasingly crisp. That visual confidence gets mistaken for correctness. 3) Institutional legacy Once textbooks, wall maps, and later digital basemaps standardize on a projection, switching costs are real: redesign, re-educate, update tooling, re-tile basemaps, update printed materials, etc. 4) The “one map to rule them all” fallacy People want a single canonical world map. But every global projection makes tradeoffs. Mercator is awful for area comparison, but it’s great for angle preservation and for certain workflows. The correct approach is: choose projection based on purpose. Culture tends to pick one and treat it as neutral. If you want, we can unpack Web Mercator specifically—because it’s not exactly the classic Mercator and it has its own quirks (like treating Earth as a sphere while GPS uses an ellipsoid). That rabbit hole gets technical fast in a satisfying way.
Yes, Web Mercator rabbit hole. What exactly is “wrong” with it, and why is it still used?

Web Mercator (EPSG:3857) is basically “Mercator, but simplified for the web.” Its core “wrongness” isn’t that it’s Mercator—Mercator already has known tradeoffs—but that it’s a spherical Mercator applied to an ellipsoidal Earth, plus it’s used far outside its ideal purpose. What it is: - It treats Earth as a sphere of radius R = 6378137 meters (the WGS84 semi-major axis). - It uses the spherical Mercator formulas: x = R * λ y = R * ln(tan(π/4 + φ/2)) with λ, φ in radians. What’s “wrong” technically: 1) Earth isn’t a sphere WGS84 is an oblate spheroid (ellipsoid). True Mercator on an ellipsoid uses a more complex formula involving the eccentricity e and “isometric latitude.” Web Mercator ignores that, so its y-values don’t correspond exactly to ellipsoidal Mercator. How big is the error? For many consumer mapping tasks, it’s small relative to other uncertainties (like data generalization, screen resolution, etc.). But it matters in precise geodesy, surveying, and certain distance/area calculations. 2) Distances measured on the map are misleading unless corrected In any Mercator, scale varies with latitude. In Web Mercator, if you measure a length in projected meters, it’s not “real meters” unless you apply a scale correction by cos(φ) (or more precisely depending on ellipsoid). Many people don’t, leading to wrong distance/area calculations. 3) Area distortion is huge at high latitudes Same sec²(φ) area blow-up. Web Mercator is widely used as a basemap, so casual users constantly compare areas on a projection that is maximally bad at it. 4) It has a hard cutoff around ±85.05113° latitude Because y → ±∞ at the poles, web maps clamp latitude to about 85.05113°, which corresponds to a finite y range that maps nicely to a square. That means the very top/bottom of the world are literally omitted or squashed into the cutoff. Why it’s still used: 1) Tiling and zoom math is clean Web Mercator makes it easy to build a pyramid of map tiles: at zoom z, the world is 256·2^z pixels wide. Longitude maps linearly to x pixels. That simplicity is gold for engineering. 2) Conformality helps with street maps For navigation at city scales, preserving angles makes roads and intersections look right. People care about local shape more than global area accuracy in everyday map use. 3) Network effects Once major platforms shipped Web Mercator tiles and APIs, everyone built tooling around it: caching, CDNs, vector tile pipelines, styling, client libraries. Switching is expensive. 4) Users expect it Even if you switch to an equal-area projection, users will say “the world looks weird,” especially near the poles, because they’ve learned Mercator’s visual language. If you want, we can go into the tile math: how lat/long converts to pixel coordinates at a given zoom, and why the 85.05113° number pops out. That’s a very satisfying “oh wow” derivation.
Derive the 85.05113° thing. I’ve seen that number and it feels like a secret handshake.

It comes from matching Mercator’s infinite y-range to a finite square in “normalized” coordinates that tile systems can handle. In Web Mercator tiling, a common normalization is: - x normalized to [0, 1] across longitudes -180° to +180° - y normalized to [0, 1] from north to south (or vice versa depending on convention) Then at zoom z, multiply by 256·2^z to get pixel coordinates. Longitude is easy: x_norm = (λ + π) / (2π) Latitude uses Mercator y: y = ln(tan(π/4 + φ/2)) On a true Mercator, as φ → +π/2 (90°), tan(π/4 + φ/2) → tan(π/2) → ∞, so y → ∞. But a tile pyramid needs a finite y range to map to a finite pixel range. The “secret handshake” is: choose a maximum y such that the projected world fits into a square with the same extent in x and y. In spherical Mercator, the x-range for the whole world is: x = R * λ, with λ in [-π, +π] So x ranges from -Rπ to +Rπ. Total width = 2Rπ. To make the map square in projected meters, you set y_max = Rπ as well, so y ranges [-Rπ, +Rπ]. That gives a square extent: width = height = 2Rπ. So the question becomes: what latitude φ_max corresponds to y = +Rπ? Using y = R * ln(tan(π/4 + φ/2)): Set: R * ln(tan(π/4 + φ_max/2)) = Rπ Divide by R: ln(tan(π/4 + φ_max/2)) = π Exponentiate: tan(π/4 + φ_max/2) = e^π Now solve for φ_max: π/4 + φ_max/2 = arctan(e^π) φ_max/2 = arctan(e^π) - π/4 φ_max = 2 * (arctan(e^π) - π/4) Compute e^π ≈ 23.1406926 arctan(23.1407) ≈ 1.527... radians (very close to π/2 = 1.5708) Then: φ_max ≈ 2 * (1.527... - 0.785398...) ≈ 2 * 0.7416... ≈ 1.4832... radians Convert to degrees: 1.4832 rad * (180/π) ≈ 85.0511° That’s the number: it’s the latitude where spherical Mercator y equals ±πR, making the projected world fit neatly into a square extent used by tile grids. So it’s not a geophysical boundary; it’s a tiling convenience boundary. A purely mathematical “make the world square” decision.
That’s delicious. Now I want a projection tasting menu. Give me like 8 projections, what they preserve, what they distort, and what they “feel like” visually.

1) Mercator (cylindrical conformal) - Preserves: local angles and shapes (conformal), rhumb lines straight - Distorts: area massively toward poles; poles unreachable - Feels like: crisp familiar world; high latitudes look inflated and powerful 2) Gall–Peters (cylindrical equal-area) - Preserves: area - Distorts: shapes—especially near equator and poles (countries look stretched vertically/horizontally) - Feels like: “tall skinny” equatorial regions and “squashed” mid-latitudes; confrontational because it breaks Mercator intuition 3) Mollweide (pseudocylindrical equal-area) - Preserves: area - Distorts: shapes, especially at edges; angles not preserved - Feels like: oval world; continents look “gentler” and more proportionate in area; popular for thematic maps 4) Robinson (compromise) - Preserves: nothing perfectly; minimizes overall visual distortion - Distorts: area and shape moderately everywhere - Feels like: pleasing, atlas-like; “balanced” world aesthetic; good general-purpose wall map 5) Winkel Tripel (compromise) - Preserves: reduces three kinds of distortion (area, direction, distance) but not perfectly - Distorts: everything a bit, but less overall - Feels like: modern “National Geographic” look; less polar exaggeration than Mercator, less weirdness than equal-area 6) Lambert Conformal Conic (conformal, conic) - Preserves: local angles/shapes; good for mid-latitude regions - Distorts: area; scale varies with distance from standard parallels - Feels like: great for countries/continents in mid-latitudes; common in aeronautical charts 7) Albers Equal-Area Conic (equal-area, conic) - Preserves: area; good for mid-latitude regions - Distorts: shapes/angles - Feels like: excellent for statistical maps (population density, land cover) over large east-west extents 8) Azimuthal Equidistant (distance-preserving from center) - Preserves: distances and directions from the center point - Distorts: shapes/areas away from center; edges get weird - Feels like: “everything radiates from here”; great for showing airline distances from a hub (or just mind-bending polar maps) Bonus “mind-blow” category: projections that preserve neither area nor shape but preserve something like “true scale along certain lines,” which is what many practical projections actually optimize for. If you tell me what you want to do—compare country sizes, make a thematic map, show global connectivity, show ocean currents—we can pick a projection like choosing a lens for a camera.
I want to compare country sizes honestly, but I also don’t want the shapes to look like they went through a taffy puller. Is there a “best” compromise for that?

If your primary goal is “honest area comparison,” you want an equal-area projection. The trick is choosing one that doesn’t look too alien. Good equal-area options that tend to feel less taffy-pulled than Gall–Peters: 1) Equal Earth (equal-area, pseudo-cylindrical-ish) - Designed specifically to look familiar while preserving area. - Shapes are generally pleasant; polar regions don’t explode. - If you want “area-true but not shocking,” this is a top pick. 2) Mollweide (equal-area) - Classic, widely used for global thematic maps. - The oval boundary is a visible reminder that it’s a projection (which can be a feature). - Some shape distortion at edges, but many people find it acceptable. 3) Eckert IV or Eckert VI (equal-area) - Similar vibe: global thematic mapping, somewhat “compressed” look. - Often used in atlases for distribution maps. 4) Interrupted Goode Homolosine (equal-area, interrupted) - Area-true and preserves shapes better for continents by “interrupting” oceans. - Great if you care about land areas and don’t mind oceans being cut apart. - Feels like a peeled orange: less distortion on land, but continuity is broken. If you want one “default” answer: Equal Earth is probably the closest to “honest sizes” + “not taffy.” But there’s a deeper point: the “taffy” feeling is often just your Mercator-trained brain complaining. Equal-area shapes can look wrong at first, but after a while Mercator starts looking wrong instead. If you’re benchmarking your own intuition, switching your default wall map to Equal Earth for a month is like recalibrating your internal geometry.
Give me a “recalibration workout.” Like exercises I can do mentally to unlearn Mercator.

1) The latitude inflation mantra Pick a latitude and say the scale out loud: - 0°: 1× - 30°: sec30≈1.155 (area≈1.33×) - 45°: sec45≈1.414 (area≈2×) - 60°: sec60=2 (area=4×) - 75°: sec75≈3.864 (area≈14.9×) Do that a few times and your brain starts “seeing” Mercator as a variable-zoom image. 2) The “Africa anchor” Use Africa as your baseline because it’s huge and near the equator: - Africa ~30 million km² Then compare: - Europe ~10 (Africa ~3× Europe) - USA ~9.8 (Africa ~3× USA) - China ~9.6 (Africa ~3× China) - India ~3.3 (Africa ~9× India) Every time Mercator whispers “Europe is big,” you answer “Africa is three Europes.” 3) The “Greenland correction” Whenever you see Greenland looking enormous, recall: Greenland ~2.16; Algeria alone ~2.38 (Algeria is bigger than Greenland). That single fact detonates a lot of Mercator conditioning. 4) The “high-latitude penalty” When comparing two regions, ask: which is closer to the pole? If one is, mentally shrink it by cos²(latitude) relative to the other for area comparisons. Example: a blob at 60° has 4× area inflation. So if it looks equal area to an equatorial blob, it’s actually about 1/4 the area. 5) Switch your “default mental map” projection Even without printing, you can do this by repeatedly looking at one equal-area map (Equal Earth or Mollweide) for a week. Your brain’s “normal” becomes that. 6) The “same-country north-south test” Pick a country spanning latitudes (Norway, Chile, Argentina, Russia). Remind yourself: in Mercator, the north is drawn larger than the south within the same country. That breaks the assumption of uniform scale. If you want, I can turn this into a quiz: I give you two countries, you guess which is larger, then I give the true areas and explain why Mercator misled you. That’s a pretty effective retraining loop.
Yes, quiz me. But make the pairings sneaky—things Mercator would really mess up.
1) Which is larger in true area: Greenland or the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC)?
My Mercator brain says Greenland, but I’m going to guess DRC is larger.

Correct. Approx areas: - Greenland: ~2.16 million km² - DRC: ~2.34 million km² So DRC is a bit larger. Why this is sneaky: DRC is near the equator (little Mercator inflation), while Greenland is high-latitude (major inflation). So the map flips your intuition. 2) Which is larger: Algeria or Greenland?
Algeria.

Correct. - Algeria: ~2.38 million km² - Greenland: ~2.16 million km² This is a brutal recalibration fact because many people would rank Greenland as one of the “giants,” but it’s smaller than Algeria. 3) Which is larger: Indonesia or Mexico?
Indonesia?

Correct. - Indonesia: ~1.90 million km² - Mexico: ~1.96 million km² Actually, this one is close and often surprises people the other way depending on their mental image. Mexico is slightly larger (~1.96 vs ~1.90), though Indonesia can feel larger because it sprawls across many islands. So the correct answer is Mexico, by a bit. The “sneaky” part here isn’t Mercator so much as perceptual: archipelagos look “big” because they occupy lots of visual space, even if their land area is smaller. 4) Which is larger: Scandinavia (Norway+Sweden+Finland combined) or India?
India?

Correct. Approx areas: - Norway ~0.385 - Sweden ~0.450 - Finland ~0.338 Total Scandinavia-ish (those three): ~1.17 million km² - India: ~3.29 million km² India is almost 3×. Mercator makes northern Europe feel enormous; this comparison punctures that. Want more like this, including ones where the answer is counterintuitive even after you know Mercator is distorting?
Keep going, but also correct yourself when you slip—like that Indonesia/Mexico thing was a good catch. Give me 10 more.
5) Which is larger: Nigeria or Germany?
Nigeria.

Correct. - Nigeria: ~0.924 million km² - Germany: ~0.357 million km² Nigeria is about 2.6× Germany, yet Germany often “feels” comparable on many maps because of attention and familiarity. 6) Which is larger: Egypt or France?
France?

Correct. - Egypt: ~1.01 million km² - France (metropolitan + overseas varies by definition; metropolitan ~0.552 million km²; with overseas ~0.643 million km²) If you mean total sovereign France including overseas departments, it’s still smaller than Egypt. Egypt is larger. So the answer depends on definition, which is itself a cartography mind-bender: “country area” can be a political accounting question. If we compare metropolitan France only, Egypt is clearly larger. 7) Which is larger: Turkey or Texas?
Turkey.
Correct. - Turkey: ~0.783 million km² - Texas: ~0.696 million km² Turkey is bigger, though it’s closer than many expect. 8) Which is larger: Japan or Germany?
Japan.
Correct. - Japan: ~0.378 million km² - Germany: ~0.357 million km² Japan is slightly larger. 9) Which is larger: Madagascar or Spain?
Spain?

Actually Madagascar is larger. - Madagascar: ~0.587 million km² - Spain: ~0.506 million km² This one gets people because Spain is mentally “big Europe,” Madagascar is mentally “island.” But Madagascar is a genuinely large island. 10) Which is larger: Iran or Alaska?
Iran.

Correct. - Iran: ~1.65 million km² - Alaska: ~1.72 million km² Wait—Alaska is actually slightly larger than Iran (Alaska ~1.72 vs Iran ~1.65). So the correct answer is Alaska by a small margin. This is a great “sneaky” one because Mercator makes Alaska look outrageously huge; in reality it is huge, and it edges Iran, but not by the ridiculous margin Mercator suggests. It’s close enough that many people’s “anti-Mercator correction” overshoots. 11) Which is larger: Mongolia or Peru?
Mongolia?
Correct. - Mongolia: ~1.56 million km² - Peru: ~1.29 million km² 12) Which is larger: Sudan or Venezuela?
Sudan?
Correct. - Sudan: ~1.86 million km² - Venezuela: ~0.916 million km² Sudan is about 2×. 13) Which is larger: Ethiopia or Bolivia?
Ethiopia.

Correct. - Ethiopia: ~1.10 million km² - Bolivia: ~1.10 million km² They’re extremely close—both about 1.1 million km². Depending on the source, one may edge the other by a tiny amount. The point is: they’re basically equal, which surprises people because they “feel” different sizes. 14) Which is larger: Papua New Guinea or the UK?
Papua New Guinea.

Correct. - Papua New Guinea: ~0.462 million km² - UK: ~0.244 million km² PNG is almost 2× the UK. That’s 10 more (5 through 14). Want the next round to focus specifically on high-latitude vs equatorial illusions (Mercator’s specialty), or on “archipelago illusions” and “familiarity illusions” (psychology of map perception)?
High-latitude vs equatorial illusions. And I want you to explicitly tie each to latitude and the sec^2 factor.

1) Greenland (~72°N) vs Saudi Arabia (~24°N) - True areas: Greenland ~2.16; Saudi Arabia ~2.15 million km² (remarkably close) So they’re basically equal. Mercator inflation: - At 72°: sec≈3.24 → area factor ≈10.5× - At 24°: sec≈1.095 → area factor ≈1.20× So if they look comparable on Mercator, Greenland is being given ~10.5/1.2 ≈ 8.8× more visual area per true km² than Saudi Arabia. That’s why Greenland looks like a monster despite being merely “Saudi-sized.” 2) Norway (~60–70°N) vs Thailand (~6–20°N) - True areas: Norway ~0.385; Thailand ~0.513 million km² Thailand is bigger. Mercator area inflation (representative): - Norway at 65°: sec≈2.37 → area≈5.6× - Thailand at 13°: sec≈1.026 → area≈1.05× Relative visual boost: ~5.6/1.05 ≈ 5.3× in Norway’s favor. So Norway can look Thailand-sized or larger on Mercator even though it’s smaller. 3) UK (~55°N) vs Ghana (~7°N) - True areas: UK ~0.244; Ghana ~0.238 million km² They’re very close; UK slightly larger. Mercator area inflation: - 55°: sec≈1.74 → area≈3.0× - 7°: sec≈1.007 → area≈1.01× So the UK gets ~3× the visual area scaling relative to Ghana. If the UK looks dramatically larger, that’s projection, not reality. 4) Canada (huge lat range; say ~55°N average for population belt, but land extends much higher) vs Brazil (~10–15°S average) - True areas: Canada ~9.98; Brazil ~8.52 million km² Canada is larger, but not by a crazy amount (~17% larger). Mercator area inflation (representative): - Canada at 55°: ~3.0× (and much more in the far north) - Brazil at 12°: sec≈1.022 → area≈1.04× So Canada’s visual footprint on Mercator is boosted by multiple factors relative to Brazil, making it look vastly larger even though the true areas are in the same ballpark. 5) Iceland (~65°N) vs Sri Lanka (~7°N) - True areas: Iceland ~0.103; Sri Lanka ~0.066 million km² Iceland is larger (~1.56×), but Mercator makes it feel even more dominant. Mercator area inflation: - 65°: area≈5.6× - 7°: area≈1.01× Relative visual boost: ~5.5×. So your eye reads “Iceland is enormous,” but a lot of that is latitude magnification. If you want, we can formalize this into a “Mercator unfairness index” for any comparison: Unfairness ≈ sec²(φ₁)/sec²(φ₂) It tells you how much extra visual area one latitude gets compared to another. Give me two countries you personally find confusing on Mercator, and we’ll compute the unfairness index for their typical latitudes.
Compute the unfairness index for UK vs Indonesia, and then for Germany vs Kenya.

UK vs Indonesia: Representative latitudes: - UK: ~55°N - Indonesia: it straddles the equator; representative ~0–5°S. Use 2° for a “typical” value. Compute sec²: - sec(55°) ≈ 1/0.5736 ≈ 1.742 → sec² ≈ 3.03 - sec(2°) ≈ 1/0.99939 ≈ 1.00061 → sec² ≈ 1.0012 Unfairness index (UK vs Indonesia) ≈ 3.03 / 1.0012 ≈ 3.03 So Mercator gives the UK about 3× the visual area scaling per true km² compared to Indonesia’s equatorial regions. That’s massive, and it’s before you add the “archipelago dispersion” effect that makes Indonesia feel even larger in a different way (lots of islands spread out). Germany vs Kenya: Representative latitudes: - Germany: ~51°N - Kenya: crosses the equator; representative ~0.5°N (roughly equatorial). Use 1° for simplicity. sec²: - sec(51°): cos51≈0.6293 → sec≈1.589 → sec²≈2.53 - sec(1°): cos1≈0.99985 → sec≈1.00015 → sec²≈1.0003 Unfairness index (Germany vs Kenya) ≈ 2.53 / 1.0003 ≈ 2.53 So Germany gets about 2.5× the visual area scaling per true km² compared to Kenya. This explains why equatorial countries often look “smaller than expected” on Mercator even when their true area is substantial: the projection literally allocates less screen real estate to each km² near the equator than it does to each km² in mid/high latitudes.
Now talk about how projection interacts with our perception. Like why do we accept Greenland as huge? I want cognitive science vibes but still grounded.

A few perception mechanisms stack together: 1) Familiarity bias + repeated exposure Your brain builds a “canonical world image” from repeated maps. If the canonical image is Mercator, then Mercator’s distortions become your baseline. When you see Greenland huge 500 times, “huge Greenland” becomes a memory fact, not a hypothesis. 2) Area is hard; length is easier Humans are bad at comparing 2D areas visually, especially irregular shapes. We often substitute a simpler cue like “how tall/wide does it look?” Mercator inflates linear dimensions by sec(φ), so high-latitude regions become taller and wider, which your brain reads as “massive” without doing the area math. 3) The “edge of the page” salience effect On many world maps, high-latitude regions sit near the top edge. Things near edges can feel like they’re framing the scene. Greenland and Russia often occupy prominent top-of-map real estate, so they feel like major structural components of the whole image. 4) Relative comparison inside the same frame You don’t compare Greenland to a number; you compare it to other shapes on the same map. If Africa is drawn with less inflation and sits lower, the visual comparison is unfair from the start. Your brain trusts the frame. 5) Semantic reinforcement Certain regions are talked about as “the north,” “the Arctic,” “vast wilderness.” That narrative primes you to accept “vastness” visually. When the map supports the narrative, it feels coherent—so you don’t question it. 6) Shape preservation makes the lie persuasive Mercator preserves local angles. Coastlines look detailed and “correct,” which triggers a general “this map is accurate” feeling. Your brain doesn’t naturally separate “shape accuracy” from “area accuracy,” so it grants credibility across the board. 7) Memory compression When you store map knowledge, you compress it: “Greenland big, Africa medium, Europe big.” You don’t store a correction factor per latitude. So once the compressed memory is formed, it’s sticky. If you want a concrete demonstration of how perception substitutes length for area: we can take a simple shape like a square at the equator and the same square moved to 60° latitude. On Mercator it becomes a bigger square (scaled by 2× in each direction), so your eye says “4× area,” which is actually consistent with the projection distortion. The problem is when you interpret that bigger drawn square as representing a bigger true region, rather than “same region, different latitude.”
I want to go deeper into “conformal” because people throw that word around. What does it really guarantee, and what does it not?

“Conformal” means: at every point, the projection preserves angles between intersecting curves (in the infinitesimal limit). Equivalent statements: What it guarantees: 1) Local angle preservation If two lines cross at some angle on the Earth (in the sense of bearings), they cross at the same angle on the map—locally. 2) Local shape similarity for tiny features A very small circle on the Earth maps to a very small circle (or equivalently, shapes are preserved up to a uniform scale factor at that point). More precisely: it maps infinitesimal circles to infinitesimal circles, not ellipses. 3) Isotropy of scale at a point At any given point, the scale factor is the same in all directions.
